Entertainment.title: The Odyssey film fans inspired to go back to the original Homer poem
Entertainment.text:
Sir Christopher Nolan's big-screen version of The Odyssey has become one of the box office hits of the year - and has also sparked interest in the original Greek myth, with book sales and audiobook listening figures rising sharply.

The director's take on Homer's epic poem took $264m (£198m) at cinema box offices globally in its opening weekend, including $52m (£39m) on giant Imax screens.

Some of those fans have been inspired to go back to the source, with sales of print editions rising by 700% in the UK last week compared with the same period in 2025.

An audio version read by Sir Stephen Fry is now in the Spotify UK audiobook top 10, while Sir Ian McKellen's narration has entered the equivalent chart in the US.

Almost 8,000 print copies of The Odyssey were sold in the UK last week, taking the total so far this year to 47,000. That's a 95% year-on-year increase, according to NielsenIQ BookData.

British chain Waterstones said there had been "phenomenal" demand, particularly for the 2017 translation by Emily Wilson, which Sir Christopher has cited as one of his sources.

"We got more stock in but we weren't anticipating quite the uplift, particularly on Emily Wilson," Waterstones chief operating officer Kate Skipper told BBC Radio 4's Today programme.

"So we're getting deliveries every day and they're flying out as soon as they land in the shops."

Wilson herself welcomed the impact of the film, but pointed out that Sir Christopher has left out some crucial elements.

"I want to celebrate the fact that so many more people are aware of The Odyssey and were brought back to the poem," she told the Associated Press, external.

"But at the same time, I had the sense that Christopher Nolan was trying to pack everything in while missing themes that were essential to the poem."

Many of those who have gone back to the ancient story are younger people who are listening to the audiobook version.

Listening figures on Spotify have risen by about 680% since the film's release on Friday, Variety reported, external - with more than 80% of the listeners being Gen Z or millennials.

In cinemas, the film's global takings include £12.6m from the UK - making it the second-biggest opening weekend in the country so far this year, behind Toy Story 5.

In the process, The Odyssey broke the record for the highest takings in an opening weekend for a film on Imax screens in the UK, according to industry publication Screen Daily, external.

The three-hour epic, which stars Matt Damon, Tom Holland and Anne Hathaway, was the first theatrical release to be shot entirely with Imax 70mm film cameras.

While there are more than 50 Imax screens in the UK, only three are showing it using 70mm film projectors - the BFI and Science Museum in London, and Printworks in Manchester.

"You do feel like you're very close to the characters, you're immersed in the action. So it is a heightened experience, I would say," film critic and Girls on Film podcast host Anna Smith told the Today programme.

Imax screenings are more expensive than regular cinema tickets, however.

"If you have the money and you've been lucky enough to book, I don't think you would regret seeing it in Imax 70mm," Metro film critic Tori Brazier said.

"But at the same time it's very expensive and we do live in a world where lots of people watch films on laptop screens and phone screens now, so if you're going to the cinema you're going to have a big-screen experience regardless."

The director's take on Homer's epic poem took $264m (£198m) at cinema box offices...

Entertainment.title: Oasis overtake The Beatles in all-time UK album chart
Entertainment.text:
Oasis's second album (What's The Story) Morning Glory? has officially overtaken the biggest-selling album by their heroes The Beatles in the list of the UK's most popular albums of all-time, external.

The Gallagher brothers' 1995 album, which contains hits like Wonderwall and Don't Look Back in Anger, is third on the all-time best-sellers list, which has been published by the Official Charts Company.

The Beatles' 1967 psychedelic masterpiece Sgt Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band, which was third on the same list a decade ago, external, has now dropped to number five.

Queen's 1981 Greatest Hits collection remains the biggest seller overall with 1992's Abba Gold in second place, while Adele's 2011 album 21 has moved up to fourth place.

The all-time list was released on Wednesday to mark the 70th anniversary of the weekly UK album chart.

In total, (What's The Story) Morning Glory? has racked up 6.2 million UK chart units - streams and sales combined - according to the Official Charts Company.

Last year, there was renewed interest in Oasis's music due to their much publicised worldwide reunion tour. The setlist lent heavily on tracks from the album, including Some Might Say and Champagne Supernova.

In 2022, Queen's compilation LP made history as the first album to pass seven million UK chart units.

Only Swedish pop maestros Abba have breached the seven million mark since, making it an elite club.

Meanwhile, Fleetwood Mac's 1977 classic Rumours and Bob Marley's best-of album Legend have both moved into the all-time top 10 over the past 10 years.

UK Official Albums Chart - all-time top 10

Greatest Hits - Queen

(What's the Story) Morning Glory - Oasis

Sgt Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band - The Beatles

Rumours - Fleetwood Mac

The Dark Side of the Moon - Pink Floyd

Thriller - Michael Jackson

Legend - Bob Marley and The Wailers

Brothers in Arms - Dire Straits

(What's the Story) Morning Glory? received surprisingly mixed reviews from the British music press when it was released in October 1995. But it went on to win best British album at the 1996 Brit Awards and has stood the test of time.

Wonderwall, from the album, became hugely popular again this summer with England fans and players singing it together at the World Cup in North America.

The booming rock ballad Don't Look Back in Anger also took on a greater significance after it was sung, impromptu, at a vigil for those who lost their lives in the 2017 Manchester Arena attack.

Becca Monahan and Chris Austin, interim co-managing directors of the Official Charts, congratulated Oasis on the album's achievements in a joint statement.

"Three decades on, its scale and influence remain extraordinary - a defining British record that continues to unite generations, fill stadiums and soundtrack lives," they noted.

Oasis Songwriter Noel Gallagher was born three days after The Beatles released their Sgt Pepper album.

The Gallagher brothers' 1995 album, which contains hits like Wonderwall and Don't Look Back in Anger, is third on...
Entertainment.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

Entertainment.title: Kaylee Hottle: 'Devastated' Millie Bobby Brown leads tributes
Entertainment.text:
Millie Bobby Brown is among the stars who have remembered the "talent" and "kindness" of US actress Kaylee Hottle, who died in a car crash aged 18.

Hottle was killed in a single-vehicle accident in Frederick, Maryland on Tuesday morning.

The young actress, who was deaf, appeared alongside Bobby Brown in the 2021 blockbuster Godzilla vs Kong.

Bobby Brown shared her sadness, alongside a black and white image of Hottle, to her Instagram story.

Over the photo, the Stranger Things star wrote: "I'm so devastated to hear this.

"You will be deeply missed Kaylee."

Hottle played a young orphan named Jia in the monster film series, and reprised the role in the 2024 sequel Godzilla x Kong: The New Empire.

British actress Rebecca Hall, who featured in both films with Hottle, paid tribute by sharing pictures of them together on set.

"My heart goes out to your family. You will be missed Kaylee," Hall said.

Fellow co-star and Mexican actress Eiza González said her "heart is absolutely broken" as she posted a photo of them posing in costume.

"I'm devastated. I adore you," wrote González.

"I know heaven has the sweetest kindest girl. We will miss you terribly."

Legendary Pictures, the production company behind the Godzilla movies, described Hottle as a "cherished member" of the production.

"Through her unforgettable portrayal of Jia, Kaylee brought extraordinary heart, humanity, and light to Godzilla, Kong, and the Monsterverse," the statement on Instagram said., external

"Her kindness, warmth, and remarkable talent touched everyone fortunate enough to work alongside her."

Oscar winning deaf actress Marlee Matlin also shared a photo to her Instagram, external of herself and fellow actress Shoshannah Stern alongside Hottle.

Matlin said she was "absolutely gutted" about the passing of "sweet Kaylee Hottle."

"May her beauty and talent be a memory for eternity."

Over the years, Hottle had worked to advocate for the deaf community.

Teri Devine, associate director of inclusion at UK charity Royal National Institute for Deaf People (RNID), told BBC Newsbeat that Hottle helped "pave the way" for greater representation.

She described her as a "passionate advocate for the deaf community and deaf actors in Hollywood" and said she used her platform to "raise awareness and inspire others to drive positive change for people who are deaf or have hearing loss".

"Representation of people who are deaf or have hearing loss in the spotlight is incredibly important," Devine added.

"Kaylee helped to improve understanding, challenge misconceptions, and prove there are no limits to what people who are deaf or have hearing loss can achieve."

The actress also set an example to young people, a spokesperson for the National Deaf Children's Society said.

Martin McLean, the charity's deputy director of policy and campaigns, said Hottle "inspired deaf children and young people by showing that there are no limits to what they can achieve".

"Positive deaf role models help deaf young people feel less alone, build confidence in their identity and ambitions, and show them that anything is possible," he added.
Entertainment.description: Millie Bobby Brown is among the stars who have remembered the "talent" and "kindness" of US actress Kaylee Hottle, who died in a car crash aged 18. Hottle was killed in a single-vehicle accident in Frederick, Maryland on Tuesday morning. The young actress, who was deaf, appeared alongside Bobby Brown in the 2021...

Entertainment.title: Gruesome images in D4vd hearing are 'worst nightmare' for Celeste Rivas Hernandez's family
Entertainment.text:
WARNING: Readers may find details in this article distressing.

Gruesome images of a teenage girl's dismembered corpse have been shown in court before her horrified parents on the opening day of a preliminary hearing in the murder case against the singer who performs under the stage name D4vd.

Prosecutors presented graphic crime scene photos showing 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez's body when it was found last year inside a Tesla registered to singer David Anthony Burke in Hollywood, Los Angeles.

A homicide detective testified that Burke, 21, had ordered a body bag, chainsaws, an inflatable pool and a shovel online in the days after Celeste's death.

Burke has pleaded not guilty to charges of murdering and sexually abusing the girl.

The grisly details had previously only been described in court filings. Tuesday's hearing marked the first time that the evidence and photographs were shown publicly in court.

Although this is not the trial itself, prosecutors are laying out the evidence they say links Burke to Celeste's death as they seek to convince the judge there is sufficient evidence for the case to proceed to trial.

Prosecutors say Burke stabbed Celeste before dismembering her body with a chainsaw to conceal the killing after years of alleged sexual abuse.

They allege he killed her because she had threatened to reveal his relationship with a minor and destroy his multi-million dollar music career.

For Celeste's parents, it was an ordeal they had prepared themselves for but could never fully anticipate, according to their lawyer.

"This is the first time they've heard this evidence in detail, and they're horrified," the family's attorney, Patrick Steinfeld, told the BBC after the court hearing.

"There was an image of Celeste's torso and her head in the trunk of the car, and the prosecutor had to ask what it was because you could not tell it was a head. It was absolutely deplorable.

"They're at a loss for words and they're just trying to do the best they can."

Steinfeld said Celeste's mother, Mercedes, had been warned in advance that graphic images would be shown.

"I told her she didn't have to be in the courtroom for this part of the hearing," he said.

"But she said, 'I want to be here to show support for Celeste.' She just put her head down and started crying."

He said he had advised both parents that once they saw the images, they would never be able to "unsee" them.

"I encouraged them to step out for a short period of time, and they decided no - they wanted to be here to show they're supporting their daughter."

"They're an extremely private family," he added. "It's a parent's worst nightmare."

The small downtown Los Angeles courtroom was packed long before Tuesday's hearing began.

Journalists, YouTubers, TikTok creators and members of the public had entered a lottery for one of the limited number of seats to watch the proceedings.

There was a palpable sense of anticipation in the room.

The constant chatter stopped when Burke was brought in.

Wearing an orange jail uniform, he was shackled at the wrists and ankles.

His handcuffs were removed, but he remained chained to his chair throughout the hearing.

He briefly smiled at members of his defence team before quietly responding to the judge with "Good morning, Your Honour", and "Yes, Your Honour".

The atmosphere shifted dramatically once Detective Joshua Byers began his testimony.

Before displaying each graphic image, prosecutor Beth Silverman repeatedly turned to Celeste's parents, warning them about what they were about to see and checking whether they wanted to look away.

At one point, Celeste's mother was comforted by supporters seated beside her.

The girl's body was discovered inside the trunk of a Tesla in September 2025 in a Hollywood tow yard.

Detective Byers described opening a cadaver bag inside the car and finding decomposing human remains, including a torso and a head, with another black refuse bag underneath containing severed limbs.

He told the court Celeste's body had decomposed so severely her face was no longer recognisable.

The court also heard evidence showing purchases delivered to Burke's address under an alias after Celeste's death, including chainsaws, body bags and an inflatable pool.

Investigators allege the items were used to dismember and conceal Celeste's body.

The court heard about tattoos prosecutors say connected Burke and Celeste.

The judge was shown photographs of a red-ink tattoo reading "Celeste" on Burke's left ring finger, as well as a "Shhh..." tattoo on his right index finger.

Prosecutors showed a photograph of Celeste with a similar "Shhh..." tattoo on her right index finger and another picture of a hand where the ring and little finger were cut off.

The preliminary hearing is expected to last several days, with prosecutors planning to call additional witnesses as they seek to establish enough evidence for the case to move to a jury trial.

Prosecutors presented...

Entertainment.title: Rebel Wilson cleared in defamation case brought by actress in her own film
Entertainment.text:
Rebel Wilson has been cleared of accusations that she defamed a young Australian actress, a Sydney court has found.

Charlotte MacInnes, who starred in Wilson's film The Deb, sued the Bridesmaids and Pitch Perfect actress for harming her reputation, over Instagram posts Wilson made in 2024 and 2025.

In the posts, Wilson accused MacInnes of retracting her complaint of sexual harassment against a film producer so she could further her career - a claim MacInnes denied.

In court on Wednesday, Justice Elizabeth Raper dismissed the case, saying the imputation that MacInnes had changed her story about the incident was not defamatory. The judge ordered MacInnes to pay Wilson's costs.

The case centres around an incident involving MacInnes and one of the producers of Wilson's film, Amanda Ghost.

In September 2023, MacInnes and Ghost went for a swim at Bondi Beach where Ghost had an allergic reaction to the cold water, prompting the pair to return to the producer's luxury apartment that she rented.

Ghost had a shower while MacInnes ran a bath for her. The young actress got into the bath to warm up with Ghost joining her shortly after. Both were wearing swimsuits.

Ghost's assistant brought hot drinks for the two women while they were in the bath and sat with them briefly.

The court heard that Wilson later spoke to MacInnes about the incident and while both sides acknowledge the conversation happened, the content of the chat remains in dispute.

Wilson says MacInnes told her the bath incident had made her feel "uncomfortable" but MacInnes denies she said this, and only described the situation as "weird" or "strange and bizarre".

MacInnes had earlier in April told the court that Wilson had "mischaracterised and exploited" an "innocent incident" and her relationship with Wilson deteriorated during filming of The Deb in the months after the bath incident.

Lawyers for MacInnes said Wilson's allegations were "malicious concoctions" and the court heard claims the Hollywood star had ordered a smear website to target Ghost, which Wilson denied.

But Wilson's lawyer argued that MacInnes - who graduated from acting school in Western Australia in 2021 - had not been harmed by the posts, pointing to a recent record deal that she had signed.

In handing down her decision, Justice Raper remarked on the bath incident.

"To say that the circumstance surrounding how they came to be in the bath are unusual would be an understatement," she told the court.

Raper also found that MacInnes had failed to establish the posts had caused or were likely to serious harm.

Wilson posted on Instagram moments after the decision, saying the "process has taught me a lot".

"It has tested me, but it has also reinforced something I've always believed. I want to live my life standing up for what I believe is right, while continuing to create work that brings people joy."

Wilson thanked her supporters and said she was "grateful this chapter has reached its conclusion". She also thanked the judge in her case and "the Australian legal system who I have the utmost respect for".

The BBC has contacted MacInnes for comment.
Entertainment.description: Rebel Wilson has been cleared of accusations that she defamed a young Australian actress, a Sydney court has found. Charlotte MacInnes, who starred in Wilson's film The Deb, sued the Bridesmaids and Pitch Perfect actress for harming her reputation, over Instagram posts Wilson made in 2024 and 2025. In the posts,...

Entertainment.title: Judge orders pause on Paramount-Warner merger after challenge from 12 states
Entertainment.text:
A federal judge on Monday ordered Paramount and Warner Bros Discovery to halt their $81bn merger for at least two weeks, allowing states that are challenging the deal more time to see their case through in court.

Twelve states, led by California, sued to block Paramount’s pending buyout of Warner last week – alleging that such a combination would “extinguish competition” in Hollywood and lead to fewer choices for consumers, particularly moviegoers and cable customers across the US.

The states’ top prosecutors called on Warner and Paramount to not close the transaction until after a court had time to “fully evaluate” their claims. And when the companies refused, they filed for a temporary restraining order – which is what district judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín granted on Monday. That opens the door to a potential preliminary injunction that the states are also seeking to effectively block the deal.

“This is a critical first win in our case to ensure this megamerger never sees the light of day,” Rob Bonta, the California attorney general, said in a statement following Monday’s order. “History tells the tale of what happens when a few people have great power over markets that are central to Americans’ lives: fewer opportunities for more people, worse products and services for all people.”

A Warner-Paramount tie-up would bring together two of the five last legacy studios in Hollywood – as well as host of TV networks, titles filling streaming libraries and news operations. That would include Warner’s HBO Max, fan favorites such as Harry Potter and even CNN coming under the same roof of Paramount-owned CBS, movies including Top Gun and the Paramount+ streaming service.

Paramount did not immediately comment on Monday’s order. But the company, which was bought out by Skydance just last year, has vowed to “vigorously defend” its Warner acquisition. Paramount previously called the states’ complaint “wrong on both the facts and the law”, maintaining that a merger would instead strengthen competition against bigger entertainment rivals. And it touted regulatory greenlights the deal has received elsewhere, including from the Trump administration last month.

The temporary restraining order granted on Monday halts the deal from progressing for at least 14 days, although the pause could be extended for up to 28 days. The court has set 3 August as a date for a hearing on the states’ preliminary injunction motion, although that schedule could also be pushed back.

Entertainment.title: ‘My life’s work was being delightful. That really hurt me’: breakout Americana star Brennan Wedl on freeing herself through song
Entertainment.text:
The songwriting on Brennan Wedl’s new album is so ironclad that I kept checking the credits to see if any of its songs were actually covers of peak Lucinda Williams tracks that I’d somehow missed. Nope: all originals by a 29-year-old songwriter whose radiant, hooky, tough Americana puts her toe-to-toe with genre godheads such as Williams circa Car Wheels on a Gravel Road, or Sheryl Crow had she grown up playing church basement punk shows; also, a vision of Courtney Love in cowboy boots.

“I love Lucinda’s very earnest way of looking at daily life, and I hoped to achieve that on this record,” says Wedl, calling from her Nashville home, surrounded by vintage magazines and tchotchkes. Wedl is warm and dudely; she answers almost every question with an exuberant “toootally!”. She cites her favourite of her new songs, the absurdly immediate Just Another Daddy Who Did Me Wrong, a bittersweet number that questions her self-destructive appeal. “It’s a very harsh look at reality, but also about how our minds can create so much fantasy around reality. I’m working on not projecting too much on reality these days.”

You could reasonably say Wedl doesn’t have too many reasons to fantasise Her new, self-titled record was produced by Waxahatchee (AKA Katie Crutchfield) and her longtime producer Brad Cook, who asked to make the album after Snail Mail’s Lindsey Jordan put them on to Wedl’s music.

“I just couldn’t wrap my head around her voice,” says Crutchfield, who had never produced before. “It’s immediately iconic, so timeless and confident – for someone who was, at the time, so undiscovered.” When Crutchfield heard an early version of Two Dollar Pistol, a loving, windows-down nudge to someone frustrated by ambition, “I was like, this person’s writing huge melodies that can be played in arenas. This is a really different level of songwriting.”

Wedl puts the fully formed nature of the album – her third solo release after more muted outings – down to never having known herself better. Combining newly written songs and others from her 20s, “it’s a lot about communicating between various versions of you, time-travel with music; conversations with the unknown, dialogue with large themes like the man, God, Jesus, the trope of ‘daddy’.”

Like many of the US’s best young alternative songwriters, Wedl comes from a religious background – but, unusually, she hasn’t rejected it wholly. She grew up in Missouri with nuns for aunts. “I’m really grateful to have seen a lot of different sides of the Catholic church,” she says. “I saw nuns devoted to service, to education. But I also saw the assault of young boys. My grandfather is a huge advocate for female priests and says we can’t keep brushing this under the rug – the church will die if they do not change their ways. So, coming from elders in my family who have really made Christianity their own, it inspired me to put a little pagan twist on it in my adult life.”

Weld discovered riot grrrl when she attended the She Rock She Rock summer camp at age 12, and found common ground between the church and punk in their impetus to come together “to build the new era of something”. Minnesota offered a great local soundtrack in the Jayhawks, the Replacements and Prince (members of the New Power Generation, his backing band, played in her grandparents’ church band), and Wedl played both with her uncle and her first band, Sex Education. (Not something they actually got much of in high school.) “As I get older and listen to these artists, it really makes me dream of the day that I’ll move back to Minnesota and be with family,” she says.

She first left home to attend Berklee College of Music in Boston, where she studied songwriting and co-founded the playful queercore outfit Dazey and the Scouts. They lasted just over two years, then became a minor pandemic TikTok sensation when a kid dancing to a Morrissey-impersonating verse in their song Wet went viral. “TikTok has its dark sides, but the discovery factor does bring people together – being able to see yourself in some kind of art or music, no matter where you are, is magic,” says Wedl.

Music also helped Wedl see herself more clearly. Her album’s rockiest moment, Pretty Little Fantasy, attacks perceptions of her as “somebody’s daughter … prim and proper”. Growing up as the eldest and only girl, she says, “I wanted to be so helpful, and I think girls are taught that a lot. But then I was introduced to riot grrrl and Marina Abramović and disgraced women throughout history, and it took most of my 20s to come to the conclusion that I was still carrying the self-imposed life’s work of being delightful, and that doesn’t help anyone – actually, it really hurts me. I think ‘delightful’ is the mask I wore out of not dealing with my own shit – and so if I was a delight, no one would have to investigate further.”

On stage, Wedl cultivated being “dead serious about having fun – the ambition to live the life I want”. It filtered into the rehearsal space, then her everyday confidence. Recently, she was reminiscing on an earlier version of her solo project. “There was an upright bass player and an electric bass player because I didn’t want to hurt someone’s feelings by saying they couldn’t play,” she says, laughing. “I’m sure that sounded horrible!” That was 10 years ago now. “Being the leader of a band is a constant crash course in people-pleasing, self-assuredness and respect, and I’m so grateful that I get to learn these huge life lessons through music.”

She also credits “various 12-step programmes” with breaking these moulds. “Twenty-one has found me sober,” she sang on her last album, 2019’s Holy Water Branch. “A lot of my life boils down to: how do my daily habits make up who I am?” she says. “When I was drinking, I was really focused on the next time I was gonna do that, who was gonna be there – this mental obsession with the wrong things.” Quitting drinking didn’t erase her problems, but it made them clearer. She realised she had become someone who wasn’t writing, neglecting “this shortcut for when I couldn’t really explain what’s on my heart to others; I could explain it to my guitar and to my journal.”

Writing, she says, “can be how we communicate with what we don’t see, what contracts we sign with ourselves. What we want to bring into this reality can be done with pen and paper a lot of the time. It’s really magical.”

Waxahatchee on Brennan Wedl

From first hearing Brennan to making the record to now, I kind of still can’t fully wrap my head around her existence. She’s unique. Three or four years ago, Lindsey Jordan [AKA Snail Mail] was at my house and we were going back and forth, playing each other stuff. She put on I Wanna Be Your TV, by Brennan, and didn’t give me any context, and I had to stop her and be like, what is this? Is it recent? Did somebody young make this or is this an old song I’ve never heard? She told me Brennan was from Nashville. I couldn’t stop listening to her – I listened to that song a million times.

Shortly thereafter, she put out some singles with [west coast label] Kill Rock Stars, a handful of which we re-recorded. Eventually, we got in contact and I got to know her, then got to watch her play music in a studio, her technique, her approach. At every turn, she impresses me – she’s so talented and so humble but not in a grating, overly precious, shucks, I shouldn’t be here kinda way. She has the absolute perfect amount of confidence. She can really write a hook. The melodies are amazing. As I got to know her better, [I saw that] her lyric writing is really strong, too. It’s all influenced by my favourite stuff: it’s Kathleen Edwards, Lucinda Williams, Tom Petty – the people’s songwriters.
Entertainment.description: The songwriting on Brennan Wedl’s new album is so ironclad that I kept checking the credits to see if any of its songs were actually covers of peak Lucinda Williams tracks that I’d somehow missed. Nope: all originals by a 29-year-old songwriter whose radiant, hooky, tough Americana puts her toe-to-toe with genre...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Saudis must recognise Israel for nuclear deal, says Trump
BreakingNewsTab.text:
The landmark nuclear deal between the US and Saudi Arabia is dependent on Riyadh joining the Abraham Accords recognising the State of Israel, President Donald Trump has said.

"The Civil Nuclear Deal (There will be no enrichment of material!)... which pertains only to non-military use... will be approved, but is totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords," Trump said in a post on Truth Social.

Trump's remarks come after criticism from experts that the deal could run the risk of future nuclear proliferation in an already unstable region.

Saudi Arabia has previously said it would not recognise Israel without the establishment of a Palestinian state.

In 2020, Trump brokered the Abraham Accords, which secured the historic normalisation of relations between Israel and two Arab nations, the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Bahrain.

Sudan, Morocco and Kazakhstan have since also signed the accords.

Saudi Arabia has not so far responded to Trump's remarks.

And it was not immediately clear if the condition of joining the Abraham Accords was stipulated in the text of the deal - which has not been released - or that Saudi Arabia had agreed to that condition, or would be forced to join the accords.

Meanwhile Israel welcomed Trump's statement.

Posting on X, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's office said Saudi Arabia joining the accords "would be an historic leap forward for peace in the Middle East".

The statement did not mention the nuclear deal. However, Israeli Economy Minister Nir Barkat said previously his country "can manage it" if Saudi Arabia had nuclear power for peaceful means.

Israel itself is widely believed to have nuclear weapons, but it neither confirms nor denies that this is the case.

The US-Saudi deal, whose signing was announced on Wednesday, is described as a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" that will provide "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy programme" by the US energy department.

The nuclear issue has been at the centre of the months-long US-Israeli conflict with Iran.
BreakingNewsTab.description: The landmark nuclear deal between the US and Saudi Arabia is dependent on Riyadh joining the Abraham Accords recognising the State of Israel, President Donald Trump has said. "The Civil Nuclear Deal (There will be no enrichment of material!)... which pertains only to non-military use... will be approved, but is...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Police failed to check Manchester synagogue attacker's phones despite arrests
BreakingNewsTab.text:
A police chief has apologised after admitting phones were twice seized from the Manchester synagogue attacker months before the car and knife assault but they were not examined.

Jihad Al-Shamie, 35, was on bail for an alleged rape when he carried out the attack on Heaton Park Synagogue in Crumpsall on 2 October 2025, in which two Jewish men were killed.

His devices, which contained evidence of his extreme views, were seized after he was arrested in February and September but not analysed.

Greater Manchester Police Chief Constable Sir Stephen Watson said he was "deeply sorry", conceding that "the information contained within these devices would have inspired further investigative action".

Sir Stephen said the end of legal proceedings against Mohammad Bashir, who has been jailed for life with a minimum term of 17 years for plotting a separate attack with Al-Shamie, meant rules around sharing information about a live criminal case had been lifted.

He said: "In conducting an internal review, it became apparent that GMP had seized Al-Shamie's mobile devices, at the point of each arrest.

"The significance of the data contents of these devices was substantial in respect of evidencing an extremist mindset.

"I am disappointed at the prospect of adding to the distress of all of those affected by the terrible events last October.

"For this, I am deeply sorry."

The apology came after Bashir's sentencing over the plot with Al-Shamie to attack the UK's Defence Academy in Shrivenham.

Details emerged in the case at Manchester Crown Court that the pair had been discussing their extremist views in WhatsApp chats, including preparing for a "war".

The court was played footage showing the moment Al-Shamie and Bashir discussed becoming martyrs.

Meanwhile, the Independent Office for Police Conduct (IOPC) confirmed it was investigating "all previous contact between Greater Manchester Police and Jihad Al-Shamie prior to the terror attack on 2 October 2025".

A spokesperson for the police watchdog said this included the actions taken by police in response to reports of crimes committed by him.

The IOPC said its investigation would consider whether GMP missed any opportunities to prevent the attack.

The watchdog said it was made aware of the information recovered from Al-Shamie's devices by GMP last week, and would review it as part of the investigation.

Sir Stephen said Al-Shamie had been arrested on 13 February 2025 on suspicion of breaching a non-molestation order; and on the 21 September 2025 on suspicion of rape.

"Regrettably, I can confirm that the significance of the information stored on Al-Shamie's devices was not realised as they had not been examined before the attack took place on the 2 October 2025," he said.

Sir Stephen said there was "little doubt" the extremist messages on the attackers phones would have led to further investigation, but the devices were not reviewed.

The inquests into the deaths of Cravitz and Daulby in the synagogue attack are set to take place in spring 2027.

The proposed scope of the inquests will include examining the radicalisation of Al-Shamie and whether the attack could have been prevented.

Sir Stephen said those inquests would examine "all aspects of the attack".

The chief constable added that it was for the IOPC and the coroner to determine the "impact" of police not reviewing the contents of Al-Shamie's devices before the attack took place.
BreakingNewsTab.description: A police chief has apologised after admitting phones were twice seized from the Manchester synagogue attacker months before the car and knife assault but they were not examined. Jihad Al-Shamie, 35, was on bail for an alleged rape when he carried out the attack on Heaton Park Synagogue in Crumpsall on 2 October...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Government to cut business rates for pubs, clubs and music venues
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Pubs, clubs and live music venues in England will be given a 20% cut to business rates from April, which the government estimates will save firms around £1,100 next year.

In his third policy announcement since becoming prime minister, Andy Burnham said: "For too long, governments have stood by while cherished venues have disappeared from our local high streets."

The cut will cost £100m and will be funded by a review of tax relief on firms such as vape shops which "do not make a positive contribution to local communities", the government said.

Hospitality bosses welcomed the support, but some pub owners said the package would not go far enough to offset the impact of cost increases elsewhere.

The 20% business rates discount will not apply to the "very largest" live music venues. Details about which businesses are eligible will be announced at Chancellor John Healey's first Budget in the autumn.

The cut is expected to benefit almost 32,000 venues, the government said.

Iain Hoskins, who owns Ma Pub Group in Liverpool, told the BBC the relief would help "chip away" at rising costs but questioned how many venues would benefit.

It could be "very meaningful", he said, but "as always, the devil is in the detail".

His pubs have previously missed out on government business rates support, and "the increases were so huge last year that now we're sort of chipping away at some of those increases".

Under previous chancellor Rachel Reeves, the government said last year it would scale back business rate discounts that had been in force since the pandemic and announced that there would be no discount at all from April this year.

That, combined with big upward adjustments to rateable values of pub premises, left landlords with the prospect of much higher rates bills.

Following criticism from the hospitality industry, the government cut business rates for pubs and music venues by 15% earlier in 2026.

The 20% discount will apply on top of the existing support.

Commenting on the cut which comes into effect next year, Steve Perez, founder of soft drinks company Global Brands and an owner of two hotels, said the announcement is "welcome… but this won't make any material difference to any pub".

UK Hospitality's chief executive, Allen Simpson, said Burham's plans are "a good start" which he said "suggests that his affection for hospitality has survived the trip down the M1".

But he added that it is "not for everybody in hospitality".

The change to business rates for some hospitality firms is the latest move in what Burnham hopes will provide "breathing space" for people and businesses.

Earlier this week, the new prime minister announced that the government would cut the 5% VAT charge on electricity bills and would cap bus fares at £2 in England outside London.

But on Thursday, Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch, said: "When I look at the plans that he has announced for the country I find myself asking 'is that it'?"

She said: "Andy Burnham's ambitions for Britain are too small."

As well as reviewing tax relief on firms such as vape shops in order to fund the rate cut, the government also said it will "crack down" on businesses selling through online marketplaces which "do not comply with their tax obligations".

The Night Time Industries Association's chief executive, Michael Kill, said the tax break could provide "meaningful relief to businesses facing sustained cost pressures".

But he said the sector is waiting for more details while questions remain about the exclusion of the largest live music venues.

The Federation of Small Businesses (FSB) said Thursday's announcement must be "a downpayment on action that reaches across the small business community".

FSB policy chief Tina McKenzie, said the plans were encouraging and fix the damage caused by past business rates decisions which are "holding back small business growth and jobs in every postcode".
BreakingNewsTab.description: Pubs, clubs and live music venues in England will be given a 20% cut to business rates from April, which the government estimates will save firms around £1,100 next year. In his third policy announcement since becoming prime minister, Andy Burnham said: "For too long, governments have stood by while cherished...

BreakingNewsTab.title: France evacuates 20,000 as wildfires spread in south-west
BreakingNewsTab.text:
About 12,000 people have been evacuated from the south-west of France and authorities are moving 8,800 others as wildfires spread in the Gironde region.

The fire brigade says about 3,100 hectares (7,660 acres) of land have already been destroyed around the Bay of Arcachon.

Many of those who had to leave the area overnight were visitors to local campsites on the Lège-Cap-Ferret peninsula and the latest evacuations also involved campsites as a precaution.

Wildfires have also broken out in Spain and Italy in recent days. Residents were evacuated near Toledo south of Madrid and a firefighter died after falling ill while tackling a blaze in Sicily.

The Gironde prefect, Sophie Brocas, said 10,000 visitors were among those evacuated from campsites overnight and the latest evacuations were going ahead "calmly" in the village of Claouey, on the shore of Arcachon Bay.

Brocas said no injuries had been reported. Some sites and roads were being closed in exposed forest areas.

"The work was relentless all night," said Gironde fire service chief Marc Vermeulen, who warned that temperatures were rising and the wind was getting stronger. "It'll peak at 20:00. Then we'll have to deal with... highly variable winds so we're currently tackling a fire that is going to be difficult to predict and that's why we cannot relax our guard."

Two local firefighters died earlier this week fighting another fire close to Bordeaux airport.

Yassmina de Freitas, 19, who lives in the coastal town of Le Porge, said she noticed smoke and the smell of burning at lunchtime on Wednesday.

By the afternoon "ash… [and] half-burned tree bark" were falling, the sky was "very dark" and the smoke was getting "thicker and thicker".

At around 20:00 local time (19:00 BST), her family was evacuated by law enforcement and taken to a gymnasium in a neighbouring town. They had to leave two of their cats and four chickens behind as they could not be found in the flurry to leave.

She said she could not sleep at all and was waking up every hour to check the fire's progress.

"I could see the number of hectares burned rising steadily," she said.

BBC South presenter Jo Kent is on holiday with her family in France, staying at the Lesley Pastourelles campsite in Claouey, next to Lège.

She said multiple people at the campsite who were very anxious about the situation had already left.

The main road down to the south of the peninsula, which is normally filled with "nose to tail traffic", was empty and the supermarket is "unnervingly quiet", Kent added.

In the Var region in south-east France, evacuated residents were told they could go home to assess potential damage to properties on Thursday morning. However, it warned people to limit their movements in the area due to the risk of the fire re-igniting as the wind picks up through the afternoon.

Several French departments have been ravaged by wildfires during a series of unusual and prolonged heatwaves that have swept across Europe this summer.

Interior Minister Laurent Nuñez paid tribute to the two firefighters who died on Tuesday near Bordeaux airport - expressing his "infinite gratitude" and "unwavering support" to their families.

In Italy, authorities have paid tribute to a firefighter who died tackling a wildfire in San Cataldo, as Sicily's regional leader Renato Schifani wished a "speedy recovery" to another firefighter involved in the same operation.

Italian Interior Minister Matteo Piantedosi paid tribute to late firefighter Alessandro Marchi who he said had died "in service to the community".

In an update on Wednesday, head of the Sicilian Civil Protection Salvo Cocina said there had been more than 350 fires on the island including 10 major ones which led to precautionary evacuations.

He said the fires had human causes - adding that firefighting has become a social and public order problem.

Meanwhile, in Spain residents have begun returning to their homes after wildfires broke out in the village of Almorox near Toledo, south of Madrid. Authorities said the fires were not yet under control and that some 890 hectares (2.200 acres) had been burned, although ongoing work had meant the perimeter of the fire had not grown.

Temperatures of at least 42C have been recorded in areas of Spain and the country's biggest wildfire this year so far has been seen this week north of the capital in Guadalajara region.

More than 35,000 hectares have burned, leaving bleak images of destroyed landscapes, however authorities there say residents are now able to return to their homes.

Countries across Europe have seen record temperatures in recent months.

A report released in April by the Copernicus Climate Change Service found at least 95% of Europe saw "above average" annual temperatures in 2025 and identified Europe as the fastest-warming continent.

Wildfire-burnt area and fire emissions both reached record levels last year, the report said.
BreakingNewsTab.description: About 12,000 people have been evacuated from the south-west of France and authorities are moving 8,800 others as wildfires spread in the Gironde region. The fire brigade says about 3,100 hectares (7,660 acres) of land have already been destroyed around the Bay of Arcachon. Many of those who had to leave the area...
BreakingNewsTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

BreakingNewsTab.title: Commonwealth Games: Katerina Johnson-Thompson out injured - why do athletes keep withdrawing?
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Olympic silver medallist Katarina Johnson-Thompson has pulled out of the Commonwealth Games because of injury.

Dina Asher-Smith and 400m Olympic silver medalist Matt Hudson-Smith, who were both set to run relays for Team England in Glasgow, have also been replaced, as has Asher-Smith's sprint team-mate Daryll Neita and 400m runner Amber Anning.

Johnson-Thompson, 33, was due to compete in her third Commonwealth Games heptathlon, having won gold on the Gold Coast in 2018 and at Birmingham in 2022.

Team England gave no details of her issue, only confirming she had been replaced in the squad by Niamh Emerson, who took bronze behind Johnson-Thompson in 2018.

The withdrawal of such high-profile names is a significant blow to the event on the day the opening ceremony takes place.

Renee Regis, Ebuka Nwokeji, Tara Simpson-Sullivan and Tom Keen have all been added to England's athletics squad in Glasgow. The track and field competition begins on Monday.

England's Olympic 800m champion Keely Hodgkinson had already skipped the event to prioritise the European Championships, which begin in Birmingham on 10 August, leading many to question the event's significance in the sporting calendar.

The squad for the European Championships is expected to be announced next week.

St Lucia's 100m Olympic champion Julien Alfred has also pulled out of the Glasgow Games, while Canadian swimmer Summer McIntosh, one of the sport's biggest stars, is favouring the Pan Pacific Swimming Championships.

With the likes of the United States, China and major European nations not part of the Commonwealths, some athletes favour other, more competitive events.

These Games were also hurt by the late confirmation of its dates and schedule, which only came in October last year. The event was originally scheduled for March, but the Australian state of Victoria pulled out as hosts in 2023.

Speaking on Thursday, organisers insisted the event would be a success despite the absence of some big names.

Scottish athletics medal hopes include new mile world record holder Josh Kerr and defending 10,000m champion Eilish McColgan, while Glasgow 2026 chief executive Phil Batty said focus should be on younger athletes.

"We have got an incredible field of athletes," he said.

"It is really important to make sure we have those athletes at the pinnacle of their career and you can see that across the sports.

"It is equally exciting to see, just like what happened in 2014 [previous Glasgow Games], which athletes this starts that breakthrough moment."

Commonwealth Sport chief executive Katie Sadlier said her organisation would have conversations with other events in the future to create a clearer window.

"This Games was a little bit of a challenge because we only had 18 months and it was really difficult to say 'where is the gap in the two-year period?'.

"As we move forward more of those conversations will happen."

Dina Asher-Smith and 400m Olympic silver medalist Matt Hudson-Smith, who were both set to run relays for Team England in Glasgow, have also been replaced, as has Asher-Smith's sprint team-mate Daryll Neita...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Police officer was violent rapist who attacked women over 14 years
BreakingNewsTab.text:
A police officer who took his own life in May was a violent rapist who carried out attacks and attempted murders of women over at least 14 years, the BBC can reveal.

PC Alan Greer's first known victim was a woman he raped in her home in Glasgow in 2012 after she had called the police to report being the victim of a crime.

The woman reported the sex attack by Greer late last year, sparking one of Police Scotland's biggest ever anti-corruption inquiries.

Detectives have so far uncovered a further eight victims, five of whom were sex workers, attacked while Greer was off duty.

It has emerged that Greer used hundreds of prostitutes throughout his police career, and it is anticipated more victims of his violence will be identified.

Assistant Chief Constable Lynn Ratcliff, gold commander for the investigation, said what the inquiry had found was "truly shocking".

She said: "This officer has clearly abused his power and has revisited someone that he's encountered in the course of his duties.

"But this victim was brave enough to come and speak to us, and that really was the trigger point for us starting our investigation.

"That's allowed us to proactively identify eight further victims."

Greer, who was born in Australia in 1978, moved to the UK when he was six.

He joined Strathclyde Police in 2009 before it merged with Police Scotland in 2013 and had 17 years of apparently unblemished service.

At first, he worked as a uniformed response officer in Glasgow before he secured a job in officer safety training, based in Coatbridge, in about 2016.

This meant he was involved in teaching probationer officers as well as refreshing more experienced officer training.

When he died in May, he was 47, with a wife and child.

Greer's family and colleagues are understood to have been "oblivious" to his violent double life.

Senior officers insisted there were no opportunities to spot his offending sooner.

It was towards the end of last year that a woman in her late thirties walked into a Glasgow police station to report a crime.

Somewhat reluctantly, she explained that she had, in 2012, attempted to report a disturbance at her home, and that uniformed officers had attended her Glasgow home.

She said the officers took her statement and left.

A few hours later one of the officers returned, alone, forced her into a sexual act and raped her.

After she told the police last year, the woman seemed to disengage from further inquiries.

The case was handed to the anti-corruption unit (ACU), who, by April, had secured a statement from her.

From this they were then able to identify the call she'd made in 2012 and could identify the officers who had attended.

They found that, in the weeks after the 2012 attack, one of those officers had repeatedly monitored the woman's crime file on the police computer for updates, perhaps to see if she had made a further disclosure about what happened to her later that day.

The PC had even carried out one of these checks while on annual leave.

Fourteen years later as anti-corruption detectives attempted to identify the woman's attacker, it would be these keystrokes, these searches in her crime file for which there was no legitimate police reason, which gave them their suspect - PC Alan Greer.

ACC Ratcliff told the BBC: "I am entirely satisfied that everything that could have been reasonably done at that time was done, and that there were no missed opportunities as a result of that information."

The information from 2018 gave detectives a second strand of evidence, and grounds to monitor every keystroke that Greer made on police computers.

They then discovered that Greer had been searching the police system for traces of a complaint from a phone number, which detectives were able to identify belonged to a Glasgow sex worker.

This was a breakthrough. This was their suspect repeating the pattern from 2012.

They now had enough evidence to put before a sheriff who authorised a warrant to search his home and seize his devices.

At dawn on 24 April, officers raided Greer's family home in what was described as a "day of executive action".

Greer appeared shocked to be arrested on suspicion of the 2012 rape.

Seven mobile phones and a laptop were seized, and Greer was brought into a police station to be fingerprinted and have his DNA taken.

He was not interviewed but was suspended from duty and released pending further enquiries.

It was only when officers started to look at his phones that the scale of Greer's offending began to emerge.

Thousands of graphic videos and images were recovered, revealing that Greer had used hundreds of prostitutes throughout his police career.

Officers are not yet even halfway through the files, but have already identified five sex workers who have been seriously sexually assaulted.

If Greer was alive, it is understood police would have sought charges of attempted murder in some of the cases, such was the level of violence used.

Three other victims have been identified, including two female police officers Greer had taken inappropriate photographs of while they were training.

As Greer's serial offending was emerging, detectives took steps to ensure his family were not in danger, and social work officers, accompanied by local police, visited his home on 5 May.

Greer died by suicide the following day.

Couzens is a former Metropolitan Police officer serving a whole-life sentence for the rape and murder of Sarah Everard.

He abused his police role to stage a fake arrest, highlighting severe vetting failures and multiple failed opportunities to uncover the risk he posed.

ACC Ratcliff said the comparison was unhelpful and that no red flags were missed.

She said: "At this stage, we've got no evidence to suggest [missed red flags] is the case, but we absolutely keep that under review because one of the things that we have to do from inquiries of this nature is make sure that we capture and we take on board the lessons learned."

The assistant chief constable insisted the vetting landscape in Police Scotland is "markedly different" to when Greer joined Strathclyde Police in 2009, and that Greer would not pass vetting checks today.

There are still 1,000s of images recovered from Greer's devices that have to be sifted through, and it is understood he used multiple pseudonyms and phones to carry out his sexual transactions.

Specialist officers have been drafted in from other units to bolster the anti-corruption unit, including from the major incident team, and the inquiry is expected to take many months.

Greer will never be brought to justice but police say they are determined to establish the full picture of his offending.

ACC Ratcliff said: "If there is anyone who believes that they may have been a victim of Alan Greer, we would urge them to have the courage and confidence to come forward and tell us about it.

"They can be assured that we will listen, and that we will investigate, and we will do our best to make sure that they get the support that they need going forward."

Production/additional reporting: Kevin Anderson and Carla Basu

If you want to contact Mark about this story, email him at mark.daly@bbc.co.uk

For support and information on the issues raised in this article, visit BBC Action Line.

PC Alan Greer's first known victim was a woman he raped in her home in Glasgow in 2012 after she had called the police to report being the victim of a...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Airport drop-off fees up by a third - here are the priciest
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Drop-off fees at the UK's major airports have risen by a third on average since last summer, new analysis has found.

The RAC motoring group looked at the cost of pulling up to the closest point to terminals at the 20 busiest airports, 16 of which had put up their prices since last year.

London airport Gatwick topped the list by charging a "farewell fee" of £10 for 10 minutes, after lifting it by £3 from last year. Stansted also charges £10 but drivers are allowed 15 minutes at its "express set down" outside departures.

Airports UK, the trade body, said that free drop-offs were available and fees were part of airports' business models and climate change objectives.

The RAC said that the price of dropping people off in "kiss-and-fly" areas rose by an average of £1.70 to £7 compared to this time last year.

London City Airport, which only started making people pay this year, is the most expensive on a per-minute basis. Its £8 charge for five minutes works out at £1.60 per minute.

There have been increases in Northern Ireland too. At Belfast International Airport, it is now £5 for 10 minutes in the drop-off zone, up £2 on last year.

In contrast, none of the top 10 airports in the EU charge for drop-offs, according to RAC research.

The RAC acknowledged UK airports generally offered other options for setting people down at locations further from the terminal, sometimes with free shuttle buses.

But the motoring organisation's senior policy officer, Rod Dennis, said "having a captive audience should be no excuse for these 'farewell fees' – especially when they're completely at odds with how major airports around the world operate".

He said drivers needed to be especially careful of barrierless "park now, pay later" drop-off zones, as they could face a parking charge notice if they failed to pay online or by phone within a certain period of time.

The Aviation Environment Federation (AEF) campaign group defended the drop-off fees.

It said there needs to be an incentive for people to use other forms of transport to reduce congestion and air pollution around airports, which it said can cause a range of childhood illnesses such as asthma.

Celeste Hicks, policy manager at AEF, said: "Any parking charges or drop-off charges could be reinvested by laying on local bus and rail services instead of lining the pockets of airport shareholders."

Airports want more people to arrive by trains and buses.

When Transport Secretary Heidi Alexander approved Gatwick's plans for a second runway, one requirement was to have 54% of passengers using public transport.

Heathrow may also have to act to try and put people off driving to get a third runway.

A London Gatwick spokesperson said drop-off charges were aimed at disincentivising "kiss-and-fly" trips and data indicated it was "influencing passengers to seek alternative options".

The airport links to 120 train stations across London and the south east, with eight local bus routes running 24/7.

Passengers can still be dropped off for free at long-stay car parks, with a free shuttle bus to the terminal and Blue Badge holders are exempt from the charge, the spokesperson said.

A spokesperson for Airports UK said where drop-off fees are charged, they are "necessary to manage congestion, and the traffic and air pollution concerns of our local communities, as well as our climate change objectives".

"These are issues that airports are mandated to manage by the government and local authorities."

But Clive Wratten, chief executive of the Business Travel Association, said fees were adding to businesses' costs.

"First it was environmental [reasons]. Then congestion management. Then funding new routes. Now apparently it is about offsetting tax rises," he said.

"The goalposts have moved so many times now that the credibility of any justification has long since gone."

The RAC motoring group looked at the cost of pulling up to the closest point to terminals at the 20 busiest airports, 16 of which had put up their prices since last year. London airport Gatwick...

BreakingNewsTab.title: UK weather: When will it rain next?
BreakingNewsTab.text:
If you cannot remember the last time it rained, you are probably not alone.

June was actually wetter than average in the UK, which might come as a surprise given our recent run of weather, but for England and Wales the majority of that rain came down in the first two weeks of the month.

Since 15 June, most of England has recorded less than 10mm of rain with large areas of central southern England seeing 3mm or less. Meanwhile, Wisley in Surrey has had no rain at all for 36 consecutive days.

Most of Wales has seen less than 25mm (1in) of rain since the middle of June, but it has been particularly dry in the north-east with Rhyl in Denbighshire recording only 3mm.

A Thames Water hosepipe ban came into force on Thursday, adding to other bans already in place for customers of other water companies across the south of the UK, as parts of southern, central and eastern England were declared to be close to drought.

Northern Ireland had a very wet June, recording 67% more rain than average, and it was also very wet in western Scotland. However, July has been much drier than average in Northern Ireland and most of Scotland, though the rain has continued in the Highlands, with Aultbea already reaching the July average rainfall, and with more on the way.

This weekend will bring a change in our weather patterns.

Normally weather systems move eastwards with time. However, the high pressure we have had for a long time now is actually going to move westward, something known as retrogression.

This will allow the jet stream to move right over the UK and steer weather fronts in, bringing some welcome rain.

The hills of Northern Ireland, northern England and north Wales will have 5-10mm of rain this weekend.

Western Scotland will be wetter, with 10-20mm over high ground.

The rain is likely to be lighter and patchier in the Midlands, southern and east England with just a few millimetres expected and some areas could miss out altogether.

Since 15 June, most of...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Why the UK is dithering over what to do about e-scooters
BreakingNewsTab.text:
When I step out of Southampton Central railway station, it takes me just seconds to spot an e-scooter. In this city, these devices have become a normal part of the transport ecosystem, along with e-bikes.

"It's just the most convenient way to commute from home to the station and back," says Keith Reed, who I speak to as he arrives at the station to catch a train to London. Keith says uses rental e-scooters twice a week. His alternative option would be his bike, but he'd then have to worry about where to leave it.

Around the city, there are parking areas for bright orange rental devices everywhere - but they're not necessarily a permanent feature. Southampton hosts one of the numerous government-backed trials around England. This one has been going on since 2021.

It also doesn't take long to find e-scooters which aren't part of the trial, but instead are owned by individuals. I see a man riding one along the pavement outside a parade of shops. He doesn't want to stop and talk.

These privately owned e-scooters are illegal to ride on public roads across the UK, but they've proliferated regardless. One expert told me the situation was a "Wild West".

Among people I spoke to in Southampton, the same concerns came up time and time again: "They're a blinking nuisance," says elderly pedestrian Fred. Another shopper, Martin, says he's "very concerned" whenever he sees them racing through precincts: "An accident's almost inevitable."

The endgame of the trials is to reach a decision on whether to make permanent hired e-scooter schemes such as the one Keith uses - and whether to bring in legislation that would legalise and regulate privately owned e-scooters too.

But six years since the trials started around England, there's still no conclusion - and as the UK government has responsibility for road traffic legislation across Great Britain, officials in Scotland and Wales say they are waiting on Westminster to act.

So why is it taking so long for the government to decide what to do, and what are the consequences of delay? What does the evidence tell us about how safe these things are?

And why are people using them in the first place - are they here to stay as a useful part of the transport offer, or will they end up being viewed as a pest on our streets?

And so far, the trials around England appear to have proved popular. According to DfT research, fom January 2022 to May 2024, 36.9 million trips were made across 30 trial areas outside of London. On average, nearly 19,500 e-scooters were available to hire every day.

Dr Yasir Ali, a reader in Transport Modelling at Loughborough University, says e-scooters are "complementary" to other modes of transport, rather than replacing them. Due to factors such as range and being exposed to the elements, they are less suitable for longer trips, rural areas with less infrastructure or in bad weather. They work best as "a first- and last-mile solution" in urban areas, to reduce reliance on cars for short trips.

Shared mobility charity CoMoUK's 2025 report suggests that for more than half of the people using rental e-scooters, the most common use was travelling to work or school. Its analysis of DfT data suggests the majority of people using hired e-scooters are male and under 35.

"Sometimes it's a bit difficult to take the bus from dot to dot", says Yan Zhang, who works at Southampton university and doesn't drive, "so for shorter distances, I'll take the scooter."

Around half of respondents to CoMoUK's online survey used it in conjunction with another mode of transport - such as riding to a railway station, like Keith.

Earlier evidence from a national evaluation of the trials published in 2022 found that although some users would have driven instead had they not had access to an e-scooter, the biggest "modal shift" - meaning switch in ways of getting around - to e-scooters was from walking. Given walking is an active mode of travel, this has raised questions over how significant the public health impact of e-scooter use really is.

And the usage of privately owned e-scooters isn't reflected in this research. The DfT has said between 760,000 and 1.2 million people in England aged 16 and over own one - despite the fact that it remains illegal to ride them in public.

In the eyes of the law, e-scooters are classed as motor vehicles. Outside of the trials, they can't be insured, and thus can only legally be ridden on private land, with the owner's permission.

The trials themselves are bound by strict rules. The devices' top speed is limited. Businesses who operate the pilots must insure them. Users must have a full or provisional driving licence, and they must not ride on the pavement. Helmets are recommended but not mandatory.

Richard Dilks, CEO of CoMoUK says "mass-scale use of something that is inherently low-carbon, low-impact, low-cost" is a "good thing" but "we also don't have rules of engagement about how they should be ridden".

And as a result of the delays in introducing regulation, Loughborogh University's Yasir Ali says "we are increasingly in a situation where the law and reality have diverged", with "the disadvantages of e-scooter use without securing the full benefits of regulation".

Back in Southampton, I meet a lady pushing a black e-scooter, who doesn't want to give her name. She says nobody told her when she bought it that it was illegal to ride on the streets.

The Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency has a unit that carries out surveillance of retailers to check they're complying with the law. But earlier this month, an investigation by the Press Association news agency discovered some retailers were advertising them for commuting purposes.

Police tell me they regularly see children illegally riding private e-scooters. Margaret Winchcomb, deputy executive director at the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety (PACTS), says some are being specifically marketed at kids.

So what can police do? Forces regularly launch crackdowns where they seize illegally used devices, and often crush them. Each year, awareness campaigns run urging parents not to buy their kids an e-scooter for Christmas. But enforcement is a challenge.

Insp Matt Boiles from Avon and Somerset's road policing team says problems arise even with the legal trial devices - for example, people riding them after drinking alcohol, which counts as drink-driving. Riding with another person on the back is another common issue.

But officers see private e-scooters being illegally ridden in public places "all the time, every single day" and they can be really hard to catch - it isn't always safe for police in a car to pursue someone on an e-scooter, he says.

Insp Boiles despairs over parents buying them for children: "We do have parents attend a police station quite a lot, getting very, very upset that their child's £500 e-scooter has been seized." But "the legislation is really, really clear that you can't have it back".

For many the biggest concern is safety.

There's a potential risk of fire if unregulated devices with lithium batteries are left to charge unattended. Most train operators have banned e-scooters on board for this reason.

But there's also the fact that riders are unprotected in the case of falling off or being hit by motorised vehicles. And - along with e-bikes - they can end up involved in collisions where pedestrians are hurt. Many trial e-scooters in the UK are restricted to 12.5mph, but privately owned scooters' speeds aren't.

Some coroners have even raised concerns. In 2022, a coroner in London called for action after the death of 14-year-old Fatima Abukah on a privately owned scooter.

According to the DfT, in 2024 - the most recent year for which figures are available - 1,390 casualties were recorded in collisions that involved e-scooter in England, Scotland and Wales, three more than in the previous 12 months. The vast majority, 1,096, were e-scooter users themselves.

Young boys and men appear to be over-represented in the stats. Of the casualties, 302 - the most of any age and sex category - were males aged 10-19.

Six deaths were recorded in 2024, unchanged from the previous year. Five of these were riders and one a pedestrian.

Although the numbers aren't conclusive, it is thought more accidents are happening on privately owned e-scooters. Winchcomb says police statistics "aren't reflective of the number of injuries".

Nonetheless, campaigners believe there is enough evidence to show that regulation is urgently needed.

Carly Calland's 14-year-old son Jacob died in March 2025 of a catastrophic head injury. He was a passenger on an e-scooter that was involved with a collision with a car.

If privately owned e-scooters are legalised for public use, Carly believes, there should be mandatory helmets, a ban on carrying passengers and penalties for parents that allow children to ride illegally.

"If Jacob was wearing a helmet that day, he would still be here," Carly, from Wythenshawe in Greater Manchester, says.

Carly is not against e-scooters. They "are really good for people to get to work, and they are eco-friendly", she tells me, but "they just need to be used in the correct way."

What happens if the current situation goes on? Her answer is emphatic: "More deaths."

Winchcomb says some of the "dithering" may be because of the potential knock-on effect of any legislation for users of mobility scooters.

There is some frustration from devolved administrations around the UK about the delays in Whitehall. The Scottish Government "will continue to urge the UK government, which holds powers on registration and licensing over off-road vehicles, to take action in this area", a spokesperson says.

The Welsh Government says it will "monitor developments on legalisation plans and consider the implications for Wales".

Northern Ireland's Department for Infrastructure says it is also monitoring the trials in England, as well as the impact of recent law changes in the Republic of Ireland.

The DfT says it says the trials are "building the real-world evidence needed to get regulation right" and it will consult on longer-term rules, including registration and licensing requirements, within the next year.

"E-scooters have real potential to improve local connectivity and cut emissions, and we want to see that potential realised safely and responsibly across the UK," a spokesperson says.

But Ali says: "The current halfway house becomes harder to justify as usage grows."

Even if legislation and associated technical standards were introduced, Richard Dilks acknowledges enforcing them would remain difficult.

But, he adds: "That problem only gets bigger if you leave it."

In this city, these devices have become a normal part of the transport ecosystem, along with e-bikes. "It's just the most convenient way to commute from home to the station and back," says Keith Reed, who I speak...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Oil prices hit $100 for the first time since May
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Oil prices hit $100 a barrel for the first time since May as the escalating conflict in the Middle East reignited fears over global energy supplies.

Brent crude - the global benchmark for oil prices - rose more than 5% on Thursday after several days of increases as the US stepped up military strikes against Iran.

Prices spiked after Houthi militia attacked oil tankers in the Red Sea, threatening a key export route that Saudi Arabia has used to bypass the Strait of Hormuz.

Gas prices have also risen steadily over the past month, with the benchmark UK gas price currently at around 150 per therm, up from around 98p per therm on 26 June.

On Thursday, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps said three tankers had abandoned an attempt to pass through Hormuz after an explosion aboard one of them caused it to catch fire.

A temporary ceasefire between the US and Iran has failed and this week US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the people in charge in Iran were "not ready to make a deal".

Brent crude - the global benchmark for oil prices - rose more than 5% on Thursday after several days of increases as the US stepped up military strikes against Iran....
