TechnologyTab.title: Ofcom unable to take further legal action against suicide forum
TechnologyTab.text:
Ofcom said it is unable to take further legal action against a pro-suicide forum which campaigners say has been linked to over 130 UK deaths.

The regulator said, external the site had blocked access for the "vast majority" of UK users, and it had "no further legal routes available" under the Online Safety Act.

But it accepted people would still be able to access the forum by using a VPN, which hides your location online.

Andy Burrows, chief executive of the Molly Rose Foundation, said the decision to take no further legal action would "result in more lost lives" and that announcing it on the day a new prime minister was named showed "an utter lack of moral courage".

In response, an Ofcom spokesperson said it had made the decision a "matter of days ago" following "months of work to exhaust all possible legal routes".

They said bereaved families were informed of the decision this morning after being contacted last week.

"In that context, we do not consider it appropriate to delay the announcement further to accommodate today's political developments," they said.

Meanwhile, Ofcom enforcement director Suzanne Cater said the regulator had used its powers "to the fullest extent possible".

"We'll continue to monitor the forum closely to check the geoblock remains in place," she said.

The geoblock means people based in the UK are unable to access the site, unless they mask their location through a VPN or similar means.

Ofcom said it was working with the government to explore whether its enforcement powers should be strengthened.

In particular, this concerns its ability to get a website blocking order through the courts, which would legally mandate internet service providers in the UK to block the website.

The regulator says it has reached the limit of what it can legally do under the Online Safety Act because the forum has implemented the geoblock.

The BBC has approached the government for comment.

The forum, which the BBC has chosen not to name due to its nature, was the first service to be investigated by the regulator under the UK's online safety laws.

BBC News investigations into the forum found young people who had been on the site had been encouraged to and given instructions on how to take their own lives.

It has been cited in multiple coroners' reports regarding the deaths of UK citizens.

In May Ofcom issued a £950,000 fine to the site, saying it did not comply with the Online Safety Act "to protect people in the UK from illegal content", and warned it was preparing to seek a court order to block access if compliance issues were not addressed.

The firm had until 12 June to pay the fine. Ofcom said it has not paid.

If a company fails to pay a fine, Ofcom can still try and recover the debt through UK-based assets - if the company has any - or through a more complex process of engaging enforcement agencies in other countries if it does not.

Sarah Ruane, director of policy at suicide prevention charity Samaritans, said it was "extremely disappointed" by the announcement.

"After months of investigating this suicide forum, and over three years since the Online Safety Act was passed, we are yet to see meaningful action against some of the most harmful sites online," she said.

"All today's announcement does is raise serious questions about whether the Online Safety Act is fit for purpose."
TechnologyTab.description: Ofcom said it is unable to take further legal action against a pro-suicide forum which campaigners say has been linked to over 130 UK deaths. The regulator said, external the site had blocked access for the "vast majority" of UK users, and it had "no further legal routes available" under the Online Safety Act. But...

TechnologyTab.title: AliExpress gets record €550m fine from EU for allowing sale of illegal products
TechnologyTab.text:
Chinese online retail giant AliExpress has been fined a record €550m (£467m) by the EU for allowing the sale of illegal products such as unsafe toys and fake clothes.

The European Commission said AliExpress has fallen short of its legal obligations "diligently assess" the risk of illegal, unsafe or fake goods on its platform.

"The spread of counterfeit clothing, unsafe toys, dangerous cosmetics and other illegal and harmful products is not an unavoidable cost of shopping online - it is a failure by AliExpress to comply with its obligations," said EU tech chief Henna Virkkunen.

AliExpress said the fine was "disproportionate" and that it would appeal.

The two-year investigation found that AliExpress's detection systems "did not work properly", with many illegal products not flagged while others that were identified remained on the site for several weeks.

The European Commission also found the company did not properly enforce penalties on traders selling illegal goods.

Furthermore, its product compliance checks could be "easily circumvented".

AliExpress, which is owned by the Chinese tech conglomerate Alibaba, has 193 million users in Europe, more than fellow Chinese online retailers Shein or Temu.

The penalty is the highest fine imposed under the Digital Services Act, which requires tech giants to do more to counter illegal and harmful content.

The act allows for fines of up to 6% of a company's revenue, but as Alibaba had a global turnover of €122bn last year, the penalty is far short of that.

AliExpress said it was surprised by and disagreed with the EU's decision.

"AliExpress has been and continues to be committed to meeting our obligations to consumers. We invest substantial resources in risk assessment and mitigation, product safety and consumer protection," it said.

"Today's decision and disproportionate fine ignores our sound risk management framework and the significant, proactive enhancements we have made. We will appeal the decision."

Under the EU's ruling, the company has to pay the penalty and present a plan by 20 October that includes what action it will take to tackle the breaches.

Earlier this year, Temu was given a €200m fine for allowing the sale of illegal products such as dangerous baby toys.
TechnologyTab.description: Chinese online retail giant AliExpress has been fined a record €550m (£467m) by the EU for allowing the sale of illegal products such as unsafe toys and fake clothes. The European Commission said AliExpress has fallen short of its legal obligations "diligently assess" the risk of illegal, unsafe or fake goods on...

TechnologyTab.title: China's Moonshot AI claims Kimi K3 can rival OpenAI and Anthropic
TechnologyTab.text:
Chinese AI start-up Moonshot has unveiled a massive new artificial intelligence model it says can rival top American firms.

The company launched Kimi K3, containing 2.8 trillion parameters, which serves as a measure of an AI's scale and processing power.

Kimi K3's full capabilities – coding, knowledge work, and reasoning – will be known when it is released as an open-source model on 27 July.

The sudden breakthrough suggests that China's tech prowess is rapidly narrowing the capabilities gap, upending long-held assumptions in the West that Chinese developers trail their American peers.

Its arrival later this month will make it the world's first open-source model in the three-trillion-parameter class that can be freely downloaded, run and customised by outside developers.

The release comes at a highly sensitive moment for the global technology sector, just weeks after the US government abruptly forced American developer Anthropic to temporarily withdraw its flagship Fable and Mythos models due to severe cybersecurity concerns.

While Washington has since lifted those restrictions, the initial move highlights how the US government now views advanced AI software as critical national infrastructure, labelling frontier models as vital national security assets subject to strict export controls.

However, the rapid arrival of Kimi K3 suggests Chinese firms are successfully bypassing these regulatory barriers and advancing independently despite US restrictions on hardware sales.

Heavily backed by domestic tech giants Alibaba and Tencent, Moonshot has quickly risen to the forefront of China's generative AI ecosystem.

In a statement the company said that K3 stands as Moonshot AI's "most capable flagship model to date".

Unlike closed, proprietary American systems from OpenAI or Anthropic, Kimi K3's open nature allows global users to modify the system for advanced reasoning and complex software development.

Moonshot AI noted that the system is uniquely built to operate with "minimal human supervision" to sustain tasks such as engineering and coding.

Third-party evaluations from Artificial Analysis and Arena.ai show the model performing on a par with leading models in the US, such as OpenAI's GPT and Anthropic's Claude.

In these independent benchmarks, external, Kimi K3 ranked first in web interface engineering, outperforming Anthropic's Fable system, external in blind human-preference tests.

While the system's massive size means running it locally requires significant computing equipment, making it open-source could heavily disrupt Silicon Valley's commercial models.

The announcement had an immediate impact on shares in Moonshot's domestic competitors Zhipu and MiniMax, which tumbled sharply in Hong Kong by about 27% and 16% respectively.

The company launched Kimi K3, containing 2.8 trillion parameters, which serves as a measure of an AI's scale and processing power. Kimi K3's full capabilities – coding, knowledge work, and...

EntertainmentTab.title: Actress Jaime Winstone joins Strictly Come Dancing 2026 line-up
EntertainmentTab.text:
Actress Jaime Winstone, known for roles in Made in Dagenham and EastEnders, has joined the line-up for Strictly Come Dancing 2026.

She is the eighth celebrity to be confirmed for this year's series, joining stars including Delta Goodrem, Lacey Turner and Dani Dyer.

Winstone, who won acclaim for her portrayal of Barbara Windsor in the BBC biopic Babs, said she was "so excited" to be joining the dance competition.

The actress said in a statement she was "no stranger to a dance floor", but added that ballroom is "a whole new world for me".

"I love a challenge though," she continued, "so can't wait to enter my showgirl era and get super sparkly!"

Winstone launched her acting career in 2004 and her film credits include Bullet Boy, Tomb Raider and Wild Bill, as well stage productions such as 2:22 A Ghost Story.

She has also appeared in TV series such as Poirot, Dead Set, Torvill & Dean, Mad Dogs, Count Abdulla and Four Lives.

After playing Windsor in Babs, Winstone stepped into the late actress's famous role of Peggy Mitchell in EastEnders, appearing in flashback specials, most recently in the soap's 40th anniversary last year.

Scroll down to see the rest of this year's line-up so far:

Known as Great Britain's most successful Paralympian of all time, Dame Sarah Storey has secured 19 gold medals across the nine Paralympic Games she competed in.

The swimmer and cyclist said embarking on the challenge is "hugely exciting" and she "can't wait to get started".

"I have always loved watching Strictly and my daughter Louisa is a very talented dancer, so to be able to learn from the best and hopefully understand even more about her world, is incredibly motivating!" she said.

The former athlete won four golds at London 2012. She started her career as a swimmer and later switched to cycling, winning 30 Paralympic medals in total.

Best is known for hosting Big Brother with AJ Odudu on ITV since 2023, and as host of the Hits Radio Breakfast Show alongside Fleur East and James Barr.

He said it was "an absolute dream" to be taking to the BBC One contest's dance floor in September, "albeit a pretty terrifying one".

Best added: "I've been lucky to do lots of live telly before, but absolutely nothing like this. And I cannot wait!

"I have no idea if I can actually dance, but bring on the sequins, bring on the quicksteps, bring on the dance floor! I'm going to try to enjoy every minute of it."

Mercer was part of the winning couple on ITV dating show Love Island last year, and also works as a model and DJ, and is an ambassador for a young people's mental health charity.

"I have grown up watching the show with my family and am so excited to now be part of the cast," he said.

Mercer also showcases his Afrobeat, hip-hop and street dance moves on his social media accounts.

"Dancing is a huge passion of mine and I can't wait to learn ballroom and do the foxtrot for the first time!" he added.

Delta Goodrem is also part of this year's celebrity list, with the Australian singer and actress becoming the third celebrity to be announced for the upcoming series.

"I've been incredibly honoured to perform on many different stages throughout my career - from TV, theatre, film sets, to touring my own shows around the world," Goodrem said.

"There is, however, one stage I've never stepped onto and that is the ballroom floor. I'm absolutely thrilled to be joining Strictly and can't wait to get started."

She rose to fame in hit Australian soap opera Neighbours and recently represented her nation in the 2026 edition of the Eurovision Song Contest.

Celebrity hair stylist Chris Appleton is also part of this year's line-up.

Appleton, best known for his work with Kim Kardashian, joins singer-songwriter Delta Goodrem, EastEnders actress Lacey Turner and reality star Dani Dyer in the battle for the Glitterball trophy.

The Leicester-born Color Wow creative director said: "I'm thrilled to be joining Strictly Come Dancing and coming home to the UK for this incredible experience."

He has also created red carpet looks for the likes of Jennifer Lopez, Katy Perry and Ariana Grande, while having his work showcased on the covers of publications including Vogue, Harper's Bazaar and Vanity Fair.

"I've always believed that the best things happen when you take a chance and try something new," added the 43-year-old, who this year released a memoir entitled Your Roots Don't Define You.

"I may know my way around a salon floor, but the dance floor is a whole different story - and I can't wait to get started."

Lacey Turner was the first contestant to be announced for this year's series, which is expected to begin in September.

She said she was "so excited to be making my way to the dance floor", adding that she was a "huge fan" of the show.

Turner, 38, is best known for her role as Stacey Slater on the BBC One soap, which she joined in 2004.

She has won a British Soap Award and National Television Award for her role, and has also starred in TV dramas including the BBC's Our Girl.

In a video posted on Instagram, Turner said she was "so excited and so terrified at the same time" to be taking part in the show.

"But I've had so many friends do Strictly, and I thought it was about time that I pluck up the courage and made my way on to the dance floor."

Josh Widdicombe, Emma Willis and Johannes Radebe will take over as Strictly presenters this year, following the departures of Tess Daly and Claudia Winkleman.

The first line-up announcements were made significantly earlier than usual - the BBC usually confirms the line-up in August.

Speaking to ITV's Good Morning Britain in June, Ballas said: "There are going to be some people on there that will make you go 'wow'."

Asked about the new presenting trio, Ballas said she was "very excited to see what they bring" to the show.

"I know they took lots of chemistry tests, as did everybody, and those chemistry tests have brought those three people together, and I believe it's absolutely unbelievable," she said.

Ballas described the number of new elements on this year's show as "quite extraordinary", but added that it will still feel familiar to viewers.

"I don't think they'll change the show too much, maybe tweaks here and there, but no major changes."

Ballas, Craig Revel Horwood, Motsi Mabuse and Anton Du Beke will all return as judges.

Regular pro dancers including Amy Dowden, Dianne Buswell, Katya Jones, Vito Coppola and Aljaz Skorjanec will all be back on the dance floor.

However, professional dancers Gorka Marquez, Nadiya Bychkova, Luba Mushtuk, Karen Hauer and Michelle Tsiakkas will not be returning this year.

EntertainmentTab.title: Jason Alexander: Seinfeld actor apologises to Courtney Stodden for 'inappropriate' sketch when she was 17
EntertainmentTab.text:
US actor Jason Alexander has apologised to reality star Courtney Stodden for an "inappropriate" comedy sketch they appeared in together when she was 17.

The Seinfeld star appeared in a Funny or Die sketch with Stodden in 2012, which parodied daytime talk shows such as Dr Phil.

The sketch saw Alexander rubbing a mobile phone on Stodden's breasts because it was the only area with "plenty of signal".

After Stodden reposted and questioned the clip earlier this week, Alexander said he was "deeply sorry" and offered her his "sincerest apologies".

Writing on Instagram,, external Stodden said: "The older I get, the more impossible this is to understand.

"During this comedy sketch, Jason Alexander repeatedly rubbed his phone across my breasts while saying there was 'no signal' in my head but 'plenty of signal' in my chest.

"Later in the sketch, he joked that when I turned 18 he'd like to take me behind the couch and 'have his way' with me."

Stodden noted that Alexander was credited as one of the writers of the episode, adding: "Looking back now, I don't see comedy. I see a 17-year-old girl whose body became part of an adult joke.

"I was a minor. I didn't have the legal power to decide whether I wanted to participate... I was the only child in the room. I felt so alone."

Stodden said she chose to speak out after reflecting: "I can't change what happened to me but I can ask myself: How did so many adults look at a minor and decide this was acceptable?"

EntertainmentTab.title: Ex-Coronation Street actor to receive 'substantial' libel damages from the Sun publisher
EntertainmentTab.text:
Former Coronation Street actor Qasim Akhtar is to receive "substantial" damages from the Sun's publisher, to settle a High Court libel claim.

Akhtar who played Zeedan Nazir in the ITV soap from 2014 to 2018 and then 2021 to 2023, sued News Group Newspapers (NGN) earlier this year.

A story in the Sun had falsely alleged Akhtar had "backed" and "teamed up with" Islamic cleric Uthman ibn Farooq, was associated with extremism, radicalisation and violence, and had "moved to Pakistan" and "trained with guns".

The newspaper previously apologised "unreservedly" to the actor for the "serious harm and distress caused to him by the publication of these false allegations".

The story, published in November, appeared in The Sun online and in print.

The Manchester-born actor, who has previously appeared in TV shows including Shameless and Waterloo Road, did not attend the court proceedings.

But his lawyer, Daniel Taylor, read out an agreed statement, saying NGN had agreed to pay "substantial damages" and Akhtar's legal costs.

"The defendant has accepted that the publication of the article caused serious harm to Mr Akhtar's reputation and also caused him great distress," he said.

"This included reputational harm to his family life and business, which he was forced to put on hold pending the matter being resolved.

"The libel was compounded by the fact that the defendant has acknowledged that it failed to obtain Mr Akhtar's response to these very serious allegations prior to publication."

EntertainmentTab.title: Bonnie Tyler funeral to be broadcast online to fans
EntertainmentTab.text:
Fans around the world will be able to watch a celebration of life service for Welsh singer Bonnie Tyler in an online broadcast.

The Total Eclipse of the Heart singer died "unexpectedly" on 8 July, aged 75, at a Portugal hospital, "as a result of the illness that she was being treated for", her family said.

The service for family and invited guests will be held at St Mary's Church, Swansea, on 17 August, and will be live streamed on the star's official website.

The cortege will then travel to her hometown of Skewen, Neath Port Talbot, travelling through the village at about 13:20 BST, an update on the website, external said.

Fans will also be able to watch the service on television screens outside the church, the update said.

Before that, her coffin will be brought to her Mumbles home on 15 August at 15:30 BST, with people invited to "pay their respects" by lining Newton Road at 15:15.

Tyler, born Gaynor Hopkins - also known for hits including Holding Out For A Hero and Here She Comes - is survived by her husband of more than 50 years, Robert Sullivan.

Oscar-winning actress and Wednesday star Catherine Zeta-Jones led the tributes to Bonnie Tyler earlier this month, saying her heart was "broken" with the news that "our dearest Bonnie Tyler has passed away".

The Prince of Wales described Tyler as a "proud Welsh icon" and Sir Cliff Richard said he was mourning "another wonderful friend gone too soon".

In May, Tyler was placed into an induced coma after having emergency intestinal surgery in Portugal.

Last month, her spokesperson said she was out of the coma but remained "very unwell and in intensive care".

Total Eclipse of the Heart, her biggest hit, topped the charts on both sides of the Atlantic.

In doing so, she became the first Welsh person to score a number one hit in the US.

EntertainmentTab.title: Molly Smith and Tom Clare: Love Island winners marry
EntertainmentTab.text:
Love Island: All Stars winners Molly Smith and Tom Clare have tied the knot.

The couple, who won the first series of the spin-off edition in 2024, announced on Instagram, external they were "just married" alongside a photo of them kissing while flower petals floated around them.

Molly, 32, wore a white short-sleeved mini dress with a matching pillbox hat and netted veil for the occassion, while Tom, 26, wore a black suit and tie.

They announced their engagement in September last year after Tom proposed in Dubai in front of the luxury hotel, Burj Al Arab, with a host of candles and flowers.

Sharing more from their marriage ceremony, Molly posted a photo to her Instagram story of her hand on top of Tom's.

The snap showed their wedding rings along with a bouquet of flowers, with the caption: "Husband and wife".

The couple first got together two years ago, when they met on season one of Love Island: All Stars.

All Stars is a spin-off of the hit dating show, which sees past contestants return for a second chance at finding love in the villa.

During the reality dating show, the pair beat Molly's ex-boyfriend Callum Jones and his partner at the time, Jessica Gale, who finished as runners-up.

Molly previously starred in series six in 2020 and left the show with Callum, who she was in a relationship with for three years before they split.

While Hyde United footballer Tom previously came third place on the second winter edition of Love Island in 2023 with former partner Samie Elishi.

Earlier this year, Tom and Molly took over hosting duties of the NearlyWeds podcast from radio presenters and reality stars Jamie Laing and Sophie Habboo.

Fellow Love Island stars were among those to congratulate the couple, including former winner Casey O'Gorman who described it as the "best day".

Sophie Piper, who came third on their All Star season, and TikTok star GK Barry also shared their support with the newly weds.

EntertainmentTab.title: The Odyssey film fans inspired to go back to the original Homer poem
EntertainmentTab.text:
Sir Christopher Nolan's big-screen version of The Odyssey has become one of the box office hits of the year - and has also sparked interest in the original Greek myth, with book sales and audiobook listening figures rising sharply.

The director's take on Homer's epic poem took $264m (£198m) at cinema box offices globally in its opening weekend, including $52m (£39m) on giant Imax screens.

Some of those fans have been inspired to go back to the source, with sales of print editions rising by 700% in the UK last week compared with the same period in 2025.

An audio version read by Sir Stephen Fry is now in the Spotify UK audiobook top 10, while Sir Ian McKellen's narration has entered the equivalent chart in the US.

Almost 8,000 print copies of The Odyssey were sold in the UK last week, taking the total so far this year to 47,000. That's a 95% year-on-year increase, according to NielsenIQ BookData.

British chain Waterstones said there had been "phenomenal" demand, particularly for the 2017 translation by Emily Wilson, which Sir Christopher has cited as one of his sources.

"We got more stock in but we weren't anticipating quite the uplift, particularly on Emily Wilson," Waterstones chief operating officer Kate Skipper told BBC Radio 4's Today programme.

"So we're getting deliveries every day and they're flying out as soon as they land in the shops."

Wilson herself welcomed the impact of the film, but pointed out that Sir Christopher has left out some crucial elements.

"I want to celebrate the fact that so many more people are aware of The Odyssey and were brought back to the poem," she told the Associated Press, external.

"But at the same time, I had the sense that Christopher Nolan was trying to pack everything in while missing themes that were essential to the poem."

Many of those who have gone back to the ancient story are younger people who are listening to the audiobook version.

Listening figures on Spotify have risen by about 680% since the film's release on Friday, Variety reported, external - with more than 80% of the listeners being Gen Z or millennials.

In cinemas, the film's global takings include £12.6m from the UK - making it the second-biggest opening weekend in the country so far this year, behind Toy Story 5.

In the process, The Odyssey broke the record for the highest takings in an opening weekend for a film on Imax screens in the UK, according to industry publication Screen Daily, external.

The three-hour epic, which stars Matt Damon, Tom Holland and Anne Hathaway, was the first theatrical release to be shot entirely with Imax 70mm film cameras.

While there are more than 50 Imax screens in the UK, only three are showing it using 70mm film projectors - the BFI and Science Museum in London, and Printworks in Manchester.

"You do feel like you're very close to the characters, you're immersed in the action. So it is a heightened experience, I would say," film critic and Girls on Film podcast host Anna Smith told the Today programme.

Imax screenings are more expensive than regular cinema tickets, however.

"If you have the money and you've been lucky enough to book, I don't think you would regret seeing it in Imax 70mm," Metro film critic Tori Brazier said.

"But at the same time it's very expensive and we do live in a world where lots of people watch films on laptop screens and phone screens now, so if you're going to the cinema you're going to have a big-screen experience regardless."

The director's take on Homer's epic poem took $264m (£198m) at cinema box offices...

EntertainmentTab.title: Oasis overtake The Beatles in all-time UK album chart
EntertainmentTab.text:
Oasis's second album (What's The Story) Morning Glory? has officially overtaken the biggest-selling album by their heroes The Beatles in the list of the UK's most popular albums of all-time, external.

The Gallagher brothers' 1995 album, which contains hits like Wonderwall and Don't Look Back in Anger, is third on the all-time best-sellers list, which has been published by the Official Charts Company.

The Beatles' 1967 psychedelic masterpiece Sgt Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band, which was third on the same list a decade ago, external, has now dropped to number five.

Queen's 1981 Greatest Hits collection remains the biggest seller overall with 1992's Abba Gold in second place, while Adele's 2011 album 21 has moved up to fourth place.

The all-time list was released on Wednesday to mark the 70th anniversary of the weekly UK album chart.

In total, (What's The Story) Morning Glory? has racked up 6.2 million UK chart units - streams and sales combined - according to the Official Charts Company.

Last year, there was renewed interest in Oasis's music due to their much publicised worldwide reunion tour. The setlist lent heavily on tracks from the album, including Some Might Say and Champagne Supernova.

In 2022, Queen's compilation LP made history as the first album to pass seven million UK chart units.

Only Swedish pop maestros Abba have breached the seven million mark since, making it an elite club.

Meanwhile, Fleetwood Mac's 1977 classic Rumours and Bob Marley's best-of album Legend have both moved into the all-time top 10 over the past 10 years.

UK Official Albums Chart - all-time top 10

Greatest Hits - Queen

(What's the Story) Morning Glory - Oasis

Sgt Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band - The Beatles

Rumours - Fleetwood Mac

The Dark Side of the Moon - Pink Floyd

Thriller - Michael Jackson

Legend - Bob Marley and The Wailers

Brothers in Arms - Dire Straits

(What's the Story) Morning Glory? received surprisingly mixed reviews from the British music press when it was released in October 1995. But it went on to win best British album at the 1996 Brit Awards and has stood the test of time.

Wonderwall, from the album, became hugely popular again this summer with England fans and players singing it together at the World Cup in North America.

The booming rock ballad Don't Look Back in Anger also took on a greater significance after it was sung, impromptu, at a vigil for those who lost their lives in the 2017 Manchester Arena attack.

Becca Monahan and Chris Austin, interim co-managing directors of the Official Charts, congratulated Oasis on the album's achievements in a joint statement.

"Three decades on, its scale and influence remain extraordinary - a defining British record that continues to unite generations, fill stadiums and soundtrack lives," they noted.

Oasis Songwriter Noel Gallagher was born three days after The Beatles released their Sgt Pepper album.

EntertainmentTab.title: Kaylee Hottle: 'Devastated' Millie Bobby Brown leads tributes
EntertainmentTab.text:
Millie Bobby Brown is among the stars who have remembered the "talent" and "kindness" of US actress Kaylee Hottle, who died in a car crash aged 18.

Hottle was killed in a single-vehicle accident in Frederick, Maryland on Tuesday morning.

The young actress, who was deaf, appeared alongside Bobby Brown in the 2021 blockbuster Godzilla vs Kong.

Bobby Brown shared her sadness, alongside a black and white image of Hottle, to her Instagram story.

Over the photo, the Stranger Things star wrote: "I'm so devastated to hear this.

"You will be deeply missed Kaylee."

Hottle played a young orphan named Jia in the monster film series, and reprised the role in the 2024 sequel Godzilla x Kong: The New Empire.

British actress Rebecca Hall, who featured in both films with Hottle, paid tribute by sharing pictures of them together on set.

"My heart goes out to your family. You will be missed Kaylee," Hall said.

Fellow co-star and Mexican actress Eiza González said her "heart is absolutely broken" as she posted a photo of them posing in costume.

"I'm devastated. I adore you," wrote González.

"I know heaven has the sweetest kindest girl. We will miss you terribly."

Legendary Pictures, the production company behind the Godzilla movies, described Hottle as a "cherished member" of the production.

"Through her unforgettable portrayal of Jia, Kaylee brought extraordinary heart, humanity, and light to Godzilla, Kong, and the Monsterverse," the statement on Instagram said., external

"Her kindness, warmth, and remarkable talent touched everyone fortunate enough to work alongside her."

Oscar winning deaf actress Marlee Matlin also shared a photo to her Instagram, external of herself and fellow actress Shoshannah Stern alongside Hottle.

Matlin said she was "absolutely gutted" about the passing of "sweet Kaylee Hottle."

"May her beauty and talent be a memory for eternity."

Over the years, Hottle had worked to advocate for the deaf community.

Teri Devine, associate director of inclusion at UK charity Royal National Institute for Deaf People (RNID), told BBC Newsbeat that Hottle helped "pave the way" for greater representation.

She described her as a "passionate advocate for the deaf community and deaf actors in Hollywood" and said she used her platform to "raise awareness and inspire others to drive positive change for people who are deaf or have hearing loss".

"Representation of people who are deaf or have hearing loss in the spotlight is incredibly important," Devine added.

"Kaylee helped to improve understanding, challenge misconceptions, and prove there are no limits to what people who are deaf or have hearing loss can achieve."

The actress also set an example to young people, a spokesperson for the National Deaf Children's Society said.

Martin McLean, the charity's deputy director of policy and campaigns, said Hottle "inspired deaf children and young people by showing that there are no limits to what they can achieve".

"Positive deaf role models help deaf young people feel less alone, build confidence in their identity and ambitions, and show them that anything is possible," he added.

Hottle was killed in a single-vehicle accident in Frederick, Maryland on Tuesday morning. The young actress, who was deaf, appeared alongside Bobby Brown in the 2021...

EntertainmentTab.title: Gruesome images in D4vd hearing are 'worst nightmare' for Celeste Rivas Hernandez's family
EntertainmentTab.text:
WARNING: Readers may find details in this article distressing.

Gruesome images of a teenage girl's dismembered corpse have been shown in court before her horrified parents on the opening day of a preliminary hearing in the murder case against the singer who performs under the stage name D4vd.

Prosecutors presented graphic crime scene photos showing 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez's body when it was found last year inside a Tesla registered to singer David Anthony Burke in Hollywood, Los Angeles.

A homicide detective testified that Burke, 21, had ordered a body bag, chainsaws, an inflatable pool and a shovel online in the days after Celeste's death.

Burke has pleaded not guilty to charges of murdering and sexually abusing the girl.

The grisly details had previously only been described in court filings. Tuesday's hearing marked the first time that the evidence and photographs were shown publicly in court.

Although this is not the trial itself, prosecutors are laying out the evidence they say links Burke to Celeste's death as they seek to convince the judge there is sufficient evidence for the case to proceed to trial.

Prosecutors say Burke stabbed Celeste before dismembering her body with a chainsaw to conceal the killing after years of alleged sexual abuse.

They allege he killed her because she had threatened to reveal his relationship with a minor and destroy his multi-million dollar music career.

For Celeste's parents, it was an ordeal they had prepared themselves for but could never fully anticipate, according to their lawyer.

"This is the first time they've heard this evidence in detail, and they're horrified," the family's attorney, Patrick Steinfeld, told the BBC after the court hearing.

"There was an image of Celeste's torso and her head in the trunk of the car, and the prosecutor had to ask what it was because you could not tell it was a head. It was absolutely deplorable.

"They're at a loss for words and they're just trying to do the best they can."

Steinfeld said Celeste's mother, Mercedes, had been warned in advance that graphic images would be shown.

"I told her she didn't have to be in the courtroom for this part of the hearing," he said.

"But she said, 'I want to be here to show support for Celeste.' She just put her head down and started crying."

He said he had advised both parents that once they saw the images, they would never be able to "unsee" them.

"I encouraged them to step out for a short period of time, and they decided no - they wanted to be here to show they're supporting their daughter."

"They're an extremely private family," he added. "It's a parent's worst nightmare."

The small downtown Los Angeles courtroom was packed long before Tuesday's hearing began.

Journalists, YouTubers, TikTok creators and members of the public had entered a lottery for one of the limited number of seats to watch the proceedings.

There was a palpable sense of anticipation in the room.

The constant chatter stopped when Burke was brought in.

Wearing an orange jail uniform, he was shackled at the wrists and ankles.

His handcuffs were removed, but he remained chained to his chair throughout the hearing.

He briefly smiled at members of his defence team before quietly responding to the judge with "Good morning, Your Honour", and "Yes, Your Honour".

The atmosphere shifted dramatically once Detective Joshua Byers began his testimony.

Before displaying each graphic image, prosecutor Beth Silverman repeatedly turned to Celeste's parents, warning them about what they were about to see and checking whether they wanted to look away.

At one point, Celeste's mother was comforted by supporters seated beside her.

The girl's body was discovered inside the trunk of a Tesla in September 2025 in a Hollywood tow yard.

Detective Byers described opening a cadaver bag inside the car and finding decomposing human remains, including a torso and a head, with another black refuse bag underneath containing severed limbs.

He told the court Celeste's body had decomposed so severely her face was no longer recognisable.

The court also heard evidence showing purchases delivered to Burke's address under an alias after Celeste's death, including chainsaws, body bags and an inflatable pool.

Investigators allege the items were used to dismember and conceal Celeste's body.

The court heard about tattoos prosecutors say connected Burke and Celeste.

The judge was shown photographs of a red-ink tattoo reading "Celeste" on Burke's left ring finger, as well as a "Shhh..." tattoo on his right index finger.

Prosecutors showed a photograph of Celeste with a similar "Shhh..." tattoo on her right index finger and another picture of a hand where the ring and little finger were cut off.

The preliminary hearing is expected to last several days, with prosecutors planning to call additional witnesses as they seek to establish enough evidence for the case to move to a jury trial.

EntertainmentTab.title: Rebel Wilson cleared in defamation case brought by actress in her own film
EntertainmentTab.text:
Rebel Wilson has been cleared of accusations that she defamed a young Australian actress, a Sydney court has found.

Charlotte MacInnes, who starred in Wilson's film The Deb, sued the Bridesmaids and Pitch Perfect actress for harming her reputation, over Instagram posts Wilson made in 2024 and 2025.

In the posts, Wilson accused MacInnes of retracting her complaint of sexual harassment against a film producer so she could further her career - a claim MacInnes denied.

In court on Wednesday, Justice Elizabeth Raper dismissed the case, saying the imputation that MacInnes had changed her story about the incident was not defamatory. The judge ordered MacInnes to pay Wilson's costs.

The case centres around an incident involving MacInnes and one of the producers of Wilson's film, Amanda Ghost.

In September 2023, MacInnes and Ghost went for a swim at Bondi Beach where Ghost had an allergic reaction to the cold water, prompting the pair to return to the producer's luxury apartment that she rented.

Ghost had a shower while MacInnes ran a bath for her. The young actress got into the bath to warm up with Ghost joining her shortly after. Both were wearing swimsuits.

Ghost's assistant brought hot drinks for the two women while they were in the bath and sat with them briefly.

The court heard that Wilson later spoke to MacInnes about the incident and while both sides acknowledge the conversation happened, the content of the chat remains in dispute.

Wilson says MacInnes told her the bath incident had made her feel "uncomfortable" but MacInnes denies she said this, and only described the situation as "weird" or "strange and bizarre".

MacInnes had earlier in April told the court that Wilson had "mischaracterised and exploited" an "innocent incident" and her relationship with Wilson deteriorated during filming of The Deb in the months after the bath incident.

Lawyers for MacInnes said Wilson's allegations were "malicious concoctions" and the court heard claims the Hollywood star had ordered a smear website to target Ghost, which Wilson denied.

But Wilson's lawyer argued that MacInnes - who graduated from acting school in Western Australia in 2021 - had not been harmed by the posts, pointing to a recent record deal that she had signed.

In handing down her decision, Justice Raper remarked on the bath incident.

"To say that the circumstance surrounding how they came to be in the bath are unusual would be an understatement," she told the court.

Raper also found that MacInnes had failed to establish the posts had caused or were likely to serious harm.

Wilson posted on Instagram moments after the decision, saying the "process has taught me a lot".

"It has tested me, but it has also reinforced something I've always believed. I want to live my life standing up for what I believe is right, while continuing to create work that brings people joy."

Wilson thanked her supporters and said she was "grateful this chapter has reached its conclusion". She also thanked the judge in her case and "the Australian legal system who I have the utmost respect for".

The BBC has contacted MacInnes for comment.

EntertainmentTab.title: Judge orders pause on Paramount-Warner merger after challenge from 12 states
EntertainmentTab.text:
A federal judge on Monday ordered Paramount and Warner Bros Discovery to halt their $81bn merger for at least two weeks, allowing states that are challenging the deal more time to see their case through in court.

Twelve states, led by California, sued to block Paramount’s pending buyout of Warner last week – alleging that such a combination would “extinguish competition” in Hollywood and lead to fewer choices for consumers, particularly moviegoers and cable customers across the US.

The states’ top prosecutors called on Warner and Paramount to not close the transaction until after a court had time to “fully evaluate” their claims. And when the companies refused, they filed for a temporary restraining order – which is what district judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín granted on Monday. That opens the door to a potential preliminary injunction that the states are also seeking to effectively block the deal.

“This is a critical first win in our case to ensure this megamerger never sees the light of day,” Rob Bonta, the California attorney general, said in a statement following Monday’s order. “History tells the tale of what happens when a few people have great power over markets that are central to Americans’ lives: fewer opportunities for more people, worse products and services for all people.”

A Warner-Paramount tie-up would bring together two of the five last legacy studios in Hollywood – as well as host of TV networks, titles filling streaming libraries and news operations. That would include Warner’s HBO Max, fan favorites such as Harry Potter and even CNN coming under the same roof of Paramount-owned CBS, movies including Top Gun and the Paramount+ streaming service.

Paramount did not immediately comment on Monday’s order. But the company, which was bought out by Skydance just last year, has vowed to “vigorously defend” its Warner acquisition. Paramount previously called the states’ complaint “wrong on both the facts and the law”, maintaining that a merger would instead strengthen competition against bigger entertainment rivals. And it touted regulatory greenlights the deal has received elsewhere, including from the Trump administration last month.

The temporary restraining order granted on Monday halts the deal from progressing for at least 14 days, although the pause could be extended for up to 28 days. The court has set 3 August as a date for a hearing on the states’ preliminary injunction motion, although that schedule could also be pushed back.
EntertainmentTab.description: A federal judge on Monday ordered Paramount and Warner Bros Discovery to halt their $81bn merger for at least two weeks, allowing states that are challenging the deal more time to see their case through in court. Twelve states, led by California, sued to block Paramount’s pending buyout of Warner last week –...
EntertainmentTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - theguardian.com

EntertainmentTab.title: ‘My life’s work was being delightful. That really hurt me’: breakout Americana star Brennan Wedl on freeing herself through song
EntertainmentTab.text:
The songwriting on Brennan Wedl’s new album is so ironclad that I kept checking the credits to see if any of its songs were actually covers of peak Lucinda Williams tracks that I’d somehow missed. Nope: all originals by a 29-year-old songwriter whose radiant, hooky, tough Americana puts her toe-to-toe with genre godheads such as Williams circa Car Wheels on a Gravel Road, or Sheryl Crow had she grown up playing church basement punk shows; also, a vision of Courtney Love in cowboy boots.

“I love Lucinda’s very earnest way of looking at daily life, and I hoped to achieve that on this record,” says Wedl, calling from her Nashville home, surrounded by vintage magazines and tchotchkes. Wedl is warm and dudely; she answers almost every question with an exuberant “toootally!”. She cites her favourite of her new songs, the absurdly immediate Just Another Daddy Who Did Me Wrong, a bittersweet number that questions her self-destructive appeal. “It’s a very harsh look at reality, but also about how our minds can create so much fantasy around reality. I’m working on not projecting too much on reality these days.”

You could reasonably say Wedl doesn’t have too many reasons to fantasise Her new, self-titled record was produced by Waxahatchee (AKA Katie Crutchfield) and her longtime producer Brad Cook, who asked to make the album after Snail Mail’s Lindsey Jordan put them on to Wedl’s music.

“I just couldn’t wrap my head around her voice,” says Crutchfield, who had never produced before. “It’s immediately iconic, so timeless and confident – for someone who was, at the time, so undiscovered.” When Crutchfield heard an early version of Two Dollar Pistol, a loving, windows-down nudge to someone frustrated by ambition, “I was like, this person’s writing huge melodies that can be played in arenas. This is a really different level of songwriting.”

Wedl puts the fully formed nature of the album – her third solo release after more muted outings – down to never having known herself better. Combining newly written songs and others from her 20s, “it’s a lot about communicating between various versions of you, time-travel with music; conversations with the unknown, dialogue with large themes like the man, God, Jesus, the trope of ‘daddy’.”

Like many of the US’s best young alternative songwriters, Wedl comes from a religious background – but, unusually, she hasn’t rejected it wholly. She grew up in Missouri with nuns for aunts. “I’m really grateful to have seen a lot of different sides of the Catholic church,” she says. “I saw nuns devoted to service, to education. But I also saw the assault of young boys. My grandfather is a huge advocate for female priests and says we can’t keep brushing this under the rug – the church will die if they do not change their ways. So, coming from elders in my family who have really made Christianity their own, it inspired me to put a little pagan twist on it in my adult life.”

Weld discovered riot grrrl when she attended the She Rock She Rock summer camp at age 12, and found common ground between the church and punk in their impetus to come together “to build the new era of something”. Minnesota offered a great local soundtrack in the Jayhawks, the Replacements and Prince (members of the New Power Generation, his backing band, played in her grandparents’ church band), and Wedl played both with her uncle and her first band, Sex Education. (Not something they actually got much of in high school.) “As I get older and listen to these artists, it really makes me dream of the day that I’ll move back to Minnesota and be with family,” she says.

She first left home to attend Berklee College of Music in Boston, where she studied songwriting and co-founded the playful queercore outfit Dazey and the Scouts. They lasted just over two years, then became a minor pandemic TikTok sensation when a kid dancing to a Morrissey-impersonating verse in their song Wet went viral. “TikTok has its dark sides, but the discovery factor does bring people together – being able to see yourself in some kind of art or music, no matter where you are, is magic,” says Wedl.

Music also helped Wedl see herself more clearly. Her album’s rockiest moment, Pretty Little Fantasy, attacks perceptions of her as “somebody’s daughter … prim and proper”. Growing up as the eldest and only girl, she says, “I wanted to be so helpful, and I think girls are taught that a lot. But then I was introduced to riot grrrl and Marina Abramović and disgraced women throughout history, and it took most of my 20s to come to the conclusion that I was still carrying the self-imposed life’s work of being delightful, and that doesn’t help anyone – actually, it really hurts me. I think ‘delightful’ is the mask I wore out of not dealing with my own shit – and so if I was a delight, no one would have to investigate further.”

On stage, Wedl cultivated being “dead serious about having fun – the ambition to live the life I want”. It filtered into the rehearsal space, then her everyday confidence. Recently, she was reminiscing on an earlier version of her solo project. “There was an upright bass player and an electric bass player because I didn’t want to hurt someone’s feelings by saying they couldn’t play,” she says, laughing. “I’m sure that sounded horrible!” That was 10 years ago now. “Being the leader of a band is a constant crash course in people-pleasing, self-assuredness and respect, and I’m so grateful that I get to learn these huge life lessons through music.”

She also credits “various 12-step programmes” with breaking these moulds. “Twenty-one has found me sober,” she sang on her last album, 2019’s Holy Water Branch. “A lot of my life boils down to: how do my daily habits make up who I am?” she says. “When I was drinking, I was really focused on the next time I was gonna do that, who was gonna be there – this mental obsession with the wrong things.” Quitting drinking didn’t erase her problems, but it made them clearer. She realised she had become someone who wasn’t writing, neglecting “this shortcut for when I couldn’t really explain what’s on my heart to others; I could explain it to my guitar and to my journal.”

Writing, she says, “can be how we communicate with what we don’t see, what contracts we sign with ourselves. What we want to bring into this reality can be done with pen and paper a lot of the time. It’s really magical.”

Waxahatchee on Brennan Wedl

From first hearing Brennan to making the record to now, I kind of still can’t fully wrap my head around her existence. She’s unique. Three or four years ago, Lindsey Jordan [AKA Snail Mail] was at my house and we were going back and forth, playing each other stuff. She put on I Wanna Be Your TV, by Brennan, and didn’t give me any context, and I had to stop her and be like, what is this? Is it recent? Did somebody young make this or is this an old song I’ve never heard? She told me Brennan was from Nashville. I couldn’t stop listening to her – I listened to that song a million times.

Shortly thereafter, she put out some singles with [west coast label] Kill Rock Stars, a handful of which we re-recorded. Eventually, we got in contact and I got to know her, then got to watch her play music in a studio, her technique, her approach. At every turn, she impresses me – she’s so talented and so humble but not in a grating, overly precious, shucks, I shouldn’t be here kinda way. She has the absolute perfect amount of confidence. She can really write a hook. The melodies are amazing. As I got to know her better, [I saw that] her lyric writing is really strong, too. It’s all influenced by my favourite stuff: it’s Kathleen Edwards, Lucinda Williams, Tom Petty – the people’s songwriters.
EntertainmentTab.description: The songwriting on Brennan Wedl’s new album is so ironclad that I kept checking the credits to see if any of its songs were actually covers of peak Lucinda Williams tracks that I’d somehow missed. Nope: all originals by a 29-year-old songwriter whose radiant, hooky, tough Americana puts her toe-to-toe with genre...
EntertainmentTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - theguardian.com

GeneralTab.title: Saudis must recognise Israel for nuclear deal, says Trump
GeneralTab.text:
The landmark nuclear deal between the US and Saudi Arabia is dependent on Riyadh joining the Abraham Accords recognising the State of Israel, President Donald Trump has said.

"The Civil Nuclear Deal (There will be no enrichment of material!)... which pertains only to non-military use... will be approved, but is totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords," Trump said in a post on Truth Social.

Trump's remarks come after criticism from experts that the deal could run the risk of future nuclear proliferation in an already unstable region.

Saudi Arabia has previously said it would not recognise Israel without the establishment of a Palestinian state.

In 2020, Trump brokered the Abraham Accords, which secured the historic normalisation of relations between Israel and two Arab nations, the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Bahrain.

Sudan, Morocco and Kazakhstan have since also signed the accords.

Saudi Arabia has not so far responded to Trump's remarks.

And it was not immediately clear if the condition of joining the Abraham Accords was stipulated in the text of the deal - which has not been released - or that Saudi Arabia had agreed to that condition, or would be forced to join the accords.

Meanwhile Israel welcomed Trump's statement.

Posting on X, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's office said Saudi Arabia joining the accords "would be an historic leap forward for peace in the Middle East".

The statement did not mention the nuclear deal. However, Israeli Economy Minister Nir Barkat said previously his country "can manage it" if Saudi Arabia had nuclear power for peaceful means.

Israel itself is widely believed to have nuclear weapons, but it neither confirms nor denies that this is the case.

The US-Saudi deal, whose signing was announced on Wednesday, is described as a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" that will provide "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy programme" by the US energy department.

The nuclear issue has been at the centre of the months-long US-Israeli conflict with Iran.

GeneralTab.title: Police failed to check Manchester synagogue attacker's phones despite arrests
GeneralTab.text:
A police chief has apologised after admitting phones were twice seized from the Manchester synagogue attacker months before the car and knife assault but they were not examined.

Jihad Al-Shamie, 35, was on bail for an alleged rape when he carried out the attack on Heaton Park Synagogue in Crumpsall on 2 October 2025, in which two Jewish men were killed.

His devices, which contained evidence of his extreme views, were seized after he was arrested in February and September but not analysed.

Greater Manchester Police Chief Constable Sir Stephen Watson said he was "deeply sorry", conceding that "the information contained within these devices would have inspired further investigative action".

Sir Stephen said the end of legal proceedings against Mohammad Bashir, who has been jailed for life with a minimum term of 17 years for plotting a separate attack with Al-Shamie, meant rules around sharing information about a live criminal case had been lifted.

He said: "In conducting an internal review, it became apparent that GMP had seized Al-Shamie's mobile devices, at the point of each arrest.

"The significance of the data contents of these devices was substantial in respect of evidencing an extremist mindset.

"I am disappointed at the prospect of adding to the distress of all of those affected by the terrible events last October.

"For this, I am deeply sorry."

The apology came after Bashir's sentencing over the plot with Al-Shamie to attack the UK's Defence Academy in Shrivenham.

Details emerged in the case at Manchester Crown Court that the pair had been discussing their extremist views in WhatsApp chats, including preparing for a "war".

The court was played footage showing the moment Al-Shamie and Bashir discussed becoming martyrs.

Meanwhile, the Independent Office for Police Conduct (IOPC) confirmed it was investigating "all previous contact between Greater Manchester Police and Jihad Al-Shamie prior to the terror attack on 2 October 2025".

A spokesperson for the police watchdog said this included the actions taken by police in response to reports of crimes committed by him.

The IOPC said its investigation would consider whether GMP missed any opportunities to prevent the attack.

The watchdog said it was made aware of the information recovered from Al-Shamie's devices by GMP last week, and would review it as part of the investigation.

Sir Stephen said Al-Shamie had been arrested on 13 February 2025 on suspicion of breaching a non-molestation order; and on the 21 September 2025 on suspicion of rape.

"Regrettably, I can confirm that the significance of the information stored on Al-Shamie's devices was not realised as they had not been examined before the attack took place on the 2 October 2025," he said.

Sir Stephen said there was "little doubt" the extremist messages on the attackers phones would have led to further investigation, but the devices were not reviewed.

The inquests into the deaths of Cravitz and Daulby in the synagogue attack are set to take place in spring 2027.

The proposed scope of the inquests will include examining the radicalisation of Al-Shamie and whether the attack could have been prevented.

Sir Stephen said those inquests would examine "all aspects of the attack".

The chief constable added that it was for the IOPC and the coroner to determine the "impact" of police not reviewing the contents of Al-Shamie's devices before the attack took place.
GeneralTab.description: A police chief has apologised after admitting phones were twice seized from the Manchester synagogue attacker months before the car and knife assault but they were not examined. Jihad Al-Shamie, 35, was on bail for an alleged rape when he carried out the attack on Heaton Park Synagogue in Crumpsall on 2 October...

GeneralTab.title: Government to cut business rates for pubs, clubs and music venues
GeneralTab.text:
Pubs, clubs and live music venues in England will be given a 20% cut to business rates from April, which the government estimates will save firms around £1,100 next year.

In his third policy announcement since becoming prime minister, Andy Burnham said: "For too long, governments have stood by while cherished venues have disappeared from our local high streets."

The cut will cost £100m and will be funded by a review of tax relief on firms such as vape shops which "do not make a positive contribution to local communities", the government said.

Hospitality bosses welcomed the support, but some pub owners said the package would not go far enough to offset the impact of cost increases elsewhere.

The 20% business rates discount will not apply to the "very largest" live music venues. Details about which businesses are eligible will be announced at Chancellor John Healey's first Budget in the autumn.

The cut is expected to benefit almost 32,000 venues, the government said.

Iain Hoskins, who owns Ma Pub Group in Liverpool, told the BBC the relief would help "chip away" at rising costs but questioned how many venues would benefit.

It could be "very meaningful", he said, but "as always, the devil is in the detail".

His pubs have previously missed out on government business rates support, and "the increases were so huge last year that now we're sort of chipping away at some of those increases".

Under previous chancellor Rachel Reeves, the government said last year it would scale back business rate discounts that had been in force since the pandemic and announced that there would be no discount at all from April this year.

That, combined with big upward adjustments to rateable values of pub premises, left landlords with the prospect of much higher rates bills.

Following criticism from the hospitality industry, the government cut business rates for pubs and music venues by 15% earlier in 2026.

The 20% discount will apply on top of the existing support.

Commenting on the cut which comes into effect next year, Steve Perez, founder of soft drinks company Global Brands and an owner of two hotels, said the announcement is "welcome… but this won't make any material difference to any pub".

UK Hospitality's chief executive, Allen Simpson, said Burham's plans are "a good start" which he said "suggests that his affection for hospitality has survived the trip down the M1".

But he added that it is "not for everybody in hospitality".

The change to business rates for some hospitality firms is the latest move in what Burnham hopes will provide "breathing space" for people and businesses.

Earlier this week, the new prime minister announced that the government would cut the 5% VAT charge on electricity bills and would cap bus fares at £2 in England outside London.

But on Thursday, Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch, said: "When I look at the plans that he has announced for the country I find myself asking 'is that it'?"

She said: "Andy Burnham's ambitions for Britain are too small."

As well as reviewing tax relief on firms such as vape shops in order to fund the rate cut, the government also said it will "crack down" on businesses selling through online marketplaces which "do not comply with their tax obligations".

The Night Time Industries Association's chief executive, Michael Kill, said the tax break could provide "meaningful relief to businesses facing sustained cost pressures".

But he said the sector is waiting for more details while questions remain about the exclusion of the largest live music venues.

The Federation of Small Businesses (FSB) said Thursday's announcement must be "a downpayment on action that reaches across the small business community".

FSB policy chief Tina McKenzie, said the plans were encouraging and fix the damage caused by past business rates decisions which are "holding back small business growth and jobs in every postcode".
GeneralTab.description: Pubs, clubs and live music venues in England will be given a 20% cut to business rates from April, which the government estimates will save firms around £1,100 next year. In his third policy announcement since becoming prime minister, Andy Burnham said: "For too long, governments have stood by while cherished...

GeneralTab.title: Gary Lineker among millionaires asking Andy Burnham to tax them more
GeneralTab.text:
Millionaires including former footballer Gary Lineker and music producer Brian Eno have written to new prime minister Andy Burnham asking to be taxed more.

In an open letter, 120 well-off Britons told Burnham: "We can afford it. We're not talking about higher taxes on those who get up and go to work for their income every day, but on the very richest whose income is derived from the wealth they hold."

Organised by Patriotic Millionaires, the letter said it would lead to a more equal society, and urged a "devolution of wealth and power from the very richest".

People can already give money or stocks voluntarily to the Treasury using a donation facility.

Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch said Lineker is "very welcome to pay more tax he can write a cheque to the Treasury, no one is stopping him."

Patriotic Millionaires support a 2% tax on wealth over £10m.

"Millionaires are a patriotic bunch," the letter states. "We love this country and we want it to succeed.

"But success requires investment and a primary source of untouched capital investment is sitting with us, in untaxed potential."

Other signatories include Richard Curtis, director of Notting Hill, and Ian Gregg, the former managing director of Greggs and son of the bakery chain's founder. Scottish crime writer Val McDermid and former financial trader-turned-equality campaigner Gary Stevenson also signed the letter.

Chief Secretary to the Treasury Emma Reynolds said she would welcome UK millionaires increasing the amount of tax they pay but said any major changes would be announced during a Budget.

"I welcome the fact that people of good means are saying that they want to pay more. They can pay more," she said and pointed to the Treasury's donation link.

The renewed call for higher tax on the wealthy follows a similar campaign in previous years.

Burnham did not rule out a wealth tax when asked about it by Lineker - formerly the BBC's highest paid presenter - a few days before he became prime minister.

He suggested he may have "to ask for a little more" tax at some point.

Lineker was paid around £1.3m a year before he left the BBC. He is worth a reported £30m.

The latest call from Patriotic Millionaires said there is a "need to embrace a new kind of devolution of wealth and power, from the very richest in order to reinvest back into our greatest asset in every region".

It added that in its own poll, the majority of millionaires wanted a higher tax on themselves.

"There are a few people left with outdated economic thinking and few others desperate to hold onto every penny they can... Those that can't see past the end of their own self interest have no place in designing a Britain for the future," the letter said.

Musician Brian Eno told BBC Radio Lincolnshire the tax system was "very generous to rich people".

"If you're wealthy you don't pay very much tax but hire a clever bunch of accountants who somehow get you off that," he said.

He also dismissed the idea that lots of millionaires would leave the UK if they were taxed too much.

"That is the story that has always been told by the rich to avoid this but in fact people don't leave that much.

"Of course a few people will," he said, but added that people don't necessarily leave other high-tax countries, such as those in Scandinavia.

He insisted most of his financial peers do want to see better roads and schools and that they don't like seeing people living in extreme poverty, and that many financially support community groups.
GeneralTab.description: Millionaires including former footballer Gary Lineker and music producer Brian Eno have written to new prime minister Andy Burnham asking to be taxed more. In an open letter, 120 well-off Britons told Burnham: "We can afford it. We're not talking about higher taxes on those who get up and go to work for their...

GeneralTab.title: Some food prices have fallen – but inflation expected to rise from here
GeneralTab.text:
Food prices are rising at their slowest rate in nearly two years, as the cost of some staples such as margarine and sugar have gone down.

Supermarket price wars have helped drive down prices in the year to June, the industry says, as retailers work to tempt customers with summer deals.

Inflation in the UK overall has fallen to 2.6% in the year to June, down from 2.8% in the year to May, according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS), driven largely by lower fuel and food prices.

June's figure will be welcomed by new Prime Minister Andy Burnham and his government, but analysts warn the fall is temporary, as higher energy prices in July are expected to push inflation back up.

Lower fuel costs - particularly lower diesel prices - also pushed inflation down, with prices at the pump falling for the first time since the start of the war in the Middle East.

Clothing costs fell as well due to the summer sales, with many retailers offering larger discounts than last year.

Food and non-alcoholic beverage inflation fell by 0.2% month-to-month, with sugar, chocolate and confectionery seeing the largest drop in price.

Looking at inflation over the year, beef and veal price inflation eased from 9.4% in the 12 months to May to 5.1% in the year to June, while edible offal - which includes things like liver, kidneys and tongue - slowed from 9.2% to 3.4% over the same period.

Some other food items were cheaper, according to ONS data. Pizza and quiches, for example, fell by 6.7% in the year to June. Margarine dropped by 1.9% in the same period.

Food inflation often has a lag of up to 13 months due to the supply chain, so any effects from the war in Iran could still be yet to come.

Fuel prices at the pump fell in June after the US and Iran agreed to halt military operations and allow the key Strait of Hormuz to re-open.

But the recent resumption of hostilities and a new jump in crude oil prices means inflation could spike again in the coming months.

The British Retail Consortium (BRC) said lower food inflation was driven by "intense competition between supermarkets".

"If retailers are to keep prices affordable for consumers in the long run, the Government needs to take practical steps to lower the everyday cost of doing business," BRC economist Harvir Dhillon said.

"Andy Burnham has taken immediate action to ease pressure on household budgets; he must now look to do the same for businesses."

The new prime minister has pledged to make the cost of living a priority for his government, and new Chancellor John Healey said the lower rate of inflation was "news families want to hear" but "there is much more to do".

The government announced on Wednesday morning that the bus fare cap in England will be brought back down to £2 in January.

That was after Burnham announced that VAT on domestic electricity bills would be scrapped for the rest of the year from October.

Healey said: "Both these changes are a win-win. They help keep inflation down, while helping people afford the essentials."

The latest inflation figure is still above the Bank of England's target of 2%, but Suren Thiru, chief economist at the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales, said a rate increase when the Bank meets next week is unlikely.

"Rate-setters may want to assess the impact of any measures announced by the new Prime Minister before deciding whether to tighten policy again," he said.

He added that rising inflation will "likely become a more notable economic headache" for Healey, "squeezing his fiscal headroom, raising borrowing costs, and increasing financial market volatility".

Yael Selfin, KPMG's chief economist, said the June figure is likely to be the lowest of the year.

Higher energy bills, brought about by a rise in Ofgem's price cap, will likely push inflation up again, she said.

"Although the impacts from the initial energy shock have so far been relatively limited, if energy prices remain high for longer, second-round effects risk feeding through into wages and more broadly across the economy."

Sarah Coles, head of personal finance at AJ Bell, said: "The markets are still only expecting a single rate hike by the end of 2026, but it's expected to hit in September, with another potentially following in February.

"It means the most generous rates are likely to edge up. If you're in the market for a new savings account, it's worth keeping your eye open for a bargain and acting fast while it lasts."

But she added: "There's miserable news for anyone in the market for a new mortgage. Mortgage rates had been falling across the board, but this week has seen them jump significantly."
GeneralTab.description: Food prices are rising at their slowest rate in nearly two years, as the cost of some staples such as margarine and sugar have gone down. Supermarket price wars have helped drive down prices in the year to June, the industry says, as retailers work to tempt customers with summer deals. Inflation in the UK overall...
